title: Generating functions and analytic properties of Apostol-type exponential B-splines
comment: 18 pages. Preprint version submitted to Calcolo
summary: Spline functions, particularly B-splines, play a fundamental role in approximation theory, numerical analysis, and spectral representations. Although generating function techniques are widely used in combinatorics and special function theory, their systematic use in spline constructions remains relatively limited. Motivated by this observation, we introduce a generating function framework for the construction and analysis of generalized exponential B-spline families associated with Apostol-Bernoulli polynomials. Using backward shift operators and operator-based representations of uniform B-splines, we construct new Apostol-Bernoulli-type B-spline functions and derive explicit generating functions, recurrence relations, and structural identities. The present approach also yields generalized exponential spline families depending on the parameters $(λ,α)$, which interpolate between classical polynomial splines, exponential splines, and Apostol-type extensions. Furthermore, by combining de Boor recurrence relations with differential operator techniques, we establish analytic recurrence formulas involving parameter derivatives of the associated spline sequences. We also investigate the Fourier transform of the generating functions and obtain explicit rational-type representations in the frequency domain, revealing a direct connection between discrete difference operators, exponential spline structures, and spectral analytic behavior. These results provide a unified analytic approach for constructing generalized spline families and suggest further applications in approximation theory, operator-based spline analysis, and generalized spectral methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a generating function framework for constructing generalized exponential B-spline families based on Apostol-Bernoulli polynomials, using backward shift operators and de Boor recurrence relations to derive explicit formulas, recurrence relations, and Fourier transforms that unify polynomial splines, exponential splines, and Apostol-type extensions.
AI.motivation: Generating function techniques are widely used in combinatorics and special function theory but remain relatively limited in spline constructions. This gap motivates the development of a systematic generating function framework for generalized exponential B-spline families.
AI.method: The authors use backward shift operators and operator-based representations of uniform B-splines to construct Apostol-Bernoulli-type B-spline functions. They combine de Boor recurrence relations with differential operator techniques to derive analytic recurrence formulas involving parameter derivatives, and investigate Fourier transforms for frequency-domain representations.
AI.result: New Apostol-Bernoulli-type B-spline functions are constructed with explicit generating functions, recurrence relations, and structural identities. Generalized exponential spline families depending on parameters (λ,α) are derived. Fourier transforms yield explicit rational-type representations linking discrete difference operators, exponential spline structures, and spectral behavior.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified analytic approach for constructing generalized spline families, with suggested applications in approximation theory, operator-based spline analysis, and generalized spectral methods.

title: Sampling Using Hybrid Stochastic Dynamics
summary: This work proposes a framework for sampling from the Gibbs distribution of a given potential using hybrid stochastic dynamics. In this framework, two distinct sampling dynamics are run in different regions of the state space. The two dynamics are coupled across the interface through natural transmission conditions that preserve the target distribution. Using a specially constructed regularization scheme, we establish an exponential rate of convergence for the hybrid dynamics to equilibrium. We also analyze the metastability properties of the hybrid dynamics in a radially symmetric landscape, showing that the hybrid scheme can improve the mean exit time. This advantage is further confirmed by the numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid stochastic dynamics framework for sampling from Gibbs distributions, using two coupled dynamics across different state space regions with natural transmission conditions, proving exponential convergence and improved metastability properties.
AI.motivation: To improve sampling efficiency from Gibbs distributions, particularly addressing metastability issues that hinder convergence to equilibrium in traditional sampling methods.
AI.method: A hybrid stochastic dynamics framework with two distinct sampling dynamics running in different regions of state space, coupled across interfaces via natural transmission conditions, with a specially constructed regularization scheme.
AI.result: Proven exponential rate of convergence for the hybrid dynamics to equilibrium; demonstrated improved mean exit time in radially symmetric metastable landscapes; confirmed by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid framework effectively improves sampling efficiency, particularly in metastable systems, offering a principled way to combine different dynamics across regions.

title: A Fast-Convergence Resolution of the Stochastic Eigenproblem Using Halley's Method and the Spectral-Chaos Approach
summary: Solving stochastic eigenvalue problems has long been essential for informed decision-making, advancing scientific knowledge, and ensuring the reliability of engineering designs and applications. This paper underscores the need to continue enhancing existing numerical methods for solving the stochastic eigenproblem in order to improve convergence rates, computational efficiency, and robustness. Specifically, we propose a novel spectral-chaos method for solving the stochastic (linear) eigenvalue problem, employing Halley's method as the root-finding algorithm to leverage its cubic convergence properties. Our method achieves maximal convergence in solving stochastic eigenvalue problems since its rate cannot be further improved using a higher-order Householder method due to the quadratic nature of the resulting system of equations. Additionally, due to the complexity of the resulting system of equations, a tensorial approach was developed to tackle the challenges associated with the dimensional multiplicity of the stochastic eigenvalue problem, without which the solution would have been intractable. The method is derived rigorously, with a detailed error analysis that highlights the benefit of using our approach when the eigenvector components are nearly known, the computational cost of the method is also rigorously presented, and an illustrative example is provided to demonstrate the implementation of the method. Subsequently, a case study is demoed to analyze the results and validate the advantages of using Halley's method over Newton's method and Monte Carlo simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel spectral-chaos method using Halley's method (cubic convergence) for solving stochastic eigenvalue problems, achieving maximal convergence rates with a tensorial approach to handle dimensional complexity.
AI.motivation: Stochastic eigenvalue problems are essential for decision-making, scientific knowledge, and engineering reliability, but existing numerical methods need improved convergence rates, computational efficiency, and robustness.
AI.method: A spectral-chaos method using Halley's method as the root-finding algorithm (cubic convergence) with a tensorial approach to manage the dimensional multiplicity of the stochastic eigenvalue problem.
AI.result: The method achieves maximal convergence (cannot be improved by higher-order Householder methods due to quadratic system nature); validated against Newton's method and Monte Carlo simulations via a case study.
AI.conclusion: Halley's method outperforms Newton's method and Monte Carlo simulations; the tensorial approach makes the otherwise intractable solution feasible; the method is particularly beneficial when eigenvector components are nearly known.

title: pyDOF: a Python library for the design of discrete forward and inverse filters
summary: In this work, we present pyDOF, a Python-based software library which provides a domain-specific framework for the design of symmetric, physical-space, forward as well as inverse discrete filters. pyDOF is based on a constrained optimisation framework developed in our previous work [1, 2]. This framework allows the user to impose a wide range of constraints on the discrete filter transfer-function such as monotonicity, positivity, value-fixing, gradient-smoothing etc. amongst many others. pyDOF additionally includes an adaptive filter stencil selection option, and a van Cittert-based inverse-filter design with a user-controlled reconstruction order. The filter coefficients are computed automatically, and saved to a plain text file which can be readily parsed by any programming language. pyDOF can be used to design a wide range of low-pass, high-pass, multi band-pass/band-stop etc. discrete filters. In addition, due to its generality and abstraction, pyDOF can be used to design specific filters for user-defined target filter transfer functions. Although developed primarily for application to computational fluid dynamics simulations, pyDOF can be used to design discrete filters for a wide range of signal processing applications.

AI.tldr: pyDOF is a Python library for designing symmetric, physical-space discrete filters (forward and inverse) using a constrained optimization framework, with applications primarily in CFD but also general signal processing.
AI.motivation: To provide a flexible, domain-specific framework for designing discrete filters with user-imposed constraints (monotonicity, positivity, etc.), addressing the need for customizable filter design in CFD and signal processing.
AI.method: Based on a constrained optimization framework from prior work, pyDOF allows imposing constraints on transfer functions (monotonicity, positivity, value-fixing, gradient-smoothing), includes adaptive filter stencil selection, van Cittert-based inverse-filter design with user-controlled reconstruction order, and outputs filter coefficients to plain text files.
AI.result: pyDOF automatically computes filter coefficients for low-pass, high-pass, multi band-pass/band-stop filters, and user-defined target transfer functions; coefficients are saved in a portable plain text format.
AI.conclusion: pyDOF offers a general, abstract, and easy-to-use tool for discrete filter design, primarily targeting CFD but extensible to broader signal processing applications.

title: The devil in the (de)tails: an improved recovery guarantee for sparse approximation
summary: Many functions exhibit approximate sparsity in their coefficients with respect to a given dictionary. In recent literature, sparse approximation in such a dictionary from i.i.d. pointwise samples, underpinned by compressed sensing, has become a powerful tool for high-dimensional function approximation. A key step in this framework is truncating the (typically countably-infinite) dictionary to a finite index set of size $n$, so that compressed sensing tools can be used to approximate the function by a sparse combination of these truncated dictionary elements. This introduces a discrete $L^2$-truncation error over the sample points, which in standard approaches, is bounded by the continuous $L^\infty$-norm. Such a deterministic, worst-case bound ignores the randomness of the sample points entirely. As a result, $n$ must be taken unnecessarily large to keep the truncation error under control, which directly inflates the size of the matrix involved in the sparse recovery algorithm and increases computational cost. In this paper, we show that by exploiting the i.i.d. structure of the sample points, the discrete $L^2$ truncation error admits a bound that instead reflects the faster decay behaviour of the continuous $L^2$-norm truncation error and yields significantly smaller truncation sets and decreased computational cost. We demonstrate this through applications to weighted Wiener spaces and anisotropic Sobolev spaces, in each case obtaining significantly smaller truncation sets than recent works. In addition, we also present an improved bound of independent interest for sparse approximation in bounded Riesz systems, where the measurement condition exhibits a smaller (and scale-invariant) dependence on the Riesz constants than in previous works.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that by exploiting i.i.d. sample structure, the discrete L² truncation error in compressed sensing-based function approximation can be bounded by the faster-decaying continuous L² norm (instead of the worst-case L∞ norm), leading to significantly smaller truncation sets and reduced computational costs.
AI.motivation: Standard compressed sensing approaches for function approximation bound the discrete L² truncation error by the continuous L∞-norm (worst-case), which ignores sample randomness and forces unnecessarily large truncation sets, increasing computational cost.
AI.method: Exploits the i.i.d. structure of sample points to bound the discrete L² truncation error by the faster-decaying continuous L²-norm truncation error, yielding smaller truncation sets. Also provides an improved bound for sparse approximation in bounded Riesz systems with a scale-invariant dependence on Riesz constants.
AI.result: Significantly smaller truncation sets and decreased computational cost, demonstrated on weighted Wiener spaces and anisotropic Sobolev spaces. Improved bound for bounded Riesz systems.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging randomness of sample points yields more efficient compressed sensing-based high-dimensional function approximation with smaller dictionaries and lower computational burden.

title: GPU-accelerated superiorization on constrained physical problems with SupPy
summary:
The superiorization method (SM) is situated between feasibility-seeking and constrained optimization. Instead of aiming at the minimum of a given objective function over a constraint set, it seeks a feasible point at which the objective function value is reduced - though not necessarily minimal - rather than hard targets, or in which a mathematically optimal solution is not strictly required. While the method has been investigated for several applications in physics, its broader use has been limited, in part due to the lack of openly available software for researchers wishing to explore it.
  In this work we apply superiorization to three problems from applied physics: seismic image reconstruction, low-dose CT reconstruction and intensity-modulated radiotherapy treatment planning. These experiments are conducted with SupPy, an open-source modularized Python toolbox developed for this work, which supports execution of feasibility-seeking algorithms and their superiorized version on both the CPU and the GPU. In all three cases the superiorized algorithms achieve favorable results compared to feasibility-seeking alone, with reduced noise in the imaging examples and lowered body dose in the radiotherapy plans. For the radiotherapy case we further observe that superiorization produces clinically viable plans on infeasible constraint sets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces superiorization as a method between feasibility-seeking and constrained optimization, and demonstrates its effectiveness using the open-source SupPy toolbox on three applied physics problems (seismic imaging, low-dose CT, radiotherapy), showing superiorized algorithms outperform feasibility-seeking alone in noise reduction and dose minimization.
AI.motivation: Superiorization has shown promise in physics applications but lacks openly available software, limiting broader adoption. The authors aim to provide a modular Python toolbox (SupPy) and demonstrate superiorization's practical value on real-world problems.
AI.method: The authors developed SupPy, an open-source modular Python toolbox supporting CPU/GPU execution. They applied superiorized versions of feasibility-seeking algorithms to three problems: seismic image reconstruction, low-dose CT reconstruction, and intensity-modulated radiotherapy planning.
AI.result: Superiorized algorithms produced favorable results over feasibility-seeking alone: reduced noise in imaging examples, lowered body dose in radiotherapy plans. Notably, superiorization generated clinically viable plans even on infeasible constraint sets for radiotherapy.
AI.conclusion: Superiorization is an effective intermediate approach between feasibility-seeking and full optimization, and the open-source SupPy toolbox makes it more accessible for researchers to apply to their own problems.

title: Single-Shot Intensity-Correlation Diffractive X-ray Imaging of ICF Plasmas
summary: X-ray radiography of inertial confinement fusion plasmas is currently limited to several-micron resolution by geometric blur, diffraction, and photon-throughput tradeoffs. We propose single-shot intensity-correlation diffractive imaging (IDI) as a lensless route to submicron plasma turbulence measurements under low-self-emission conditions. Rather than relying on physical apertures, IDI reconstructs plasma morphology by Fourier transforming the spatial correlations of chaotic far-field speckles via the Hanbury Brown-Twiss effect. The Fourier phase is retrieved by applying bispectral closure-phase constraints derived from third-order intensity correlations. We demonstrate this submicron capability in a numerical simulation using a $50~\mathrm{keV}$ x-ray probe scattered by a spiral plasma structure.

AI.tldr: Proposes single-shot intensity-correlation diffractive imaging (IDI) for submicron resolution in ICF plasma radiography, using Hanbury Brown-Twiss correlations and bispectral phase retrieval, demonstrated numerically with a 50 keV x-ray probe on a spiral plasma structure.
AI.motivation: Current X-ray radiography of ICF plasmas is limited to several-micron resolution due to geometric blur, diffraction, and photon-throughput tradeoffs, motivating a need for submicron plasma turbulence measurements under low-self-emission conditions.
AI.method: Lensless IDI reconstructs plasma morphology by Fourier transforming spatial correlations of chaotic far-field speckles (Hanbury Brown-Twiss effect), with Fourier phase retrieved via bispectral closure-phase constraints from third-order intensity correlations.
AI.result: Demonstrates submicron imaging capability in a numerical simulation using a 50 keV x-ray probe scattered by a spiral plasma structure.
AI.conclusion: IDI offers a promising lensless route to surpass the current resolution limits in ICF plasma radiography, enabling submicron turbulence measurements.

title: Global properties of the differential complex associated to closed, nonsingular $1$-forms on compact manifolds
summary: Given a closed, real, non-singular 1-form on a compact manifold $Ω$, global properties of the associated differential complex are studied. We completely characterize global solvability in the first and last levels of the complex. Furthermore, in the particular case where the $1$-form is rational, we prove global solvability for every degree and give a complete description of the cohomology spaces. Finally, a complete characterization for global hypoellipticity is obtained, building on the work of A. Meziani (Comm. PDE., 2002). In all cases, it is shown that the conditions depend exclusively on the arithmetic nature of the form's periods.

AI.tldr: This paper studies global properties of differential complexes associated with closed, real, non-singular 1-forms on compact manifolds, characterizing global solvability and hypoellipticity based on the arithmetic nature of the form's periods.
AI.motivation: To understand how the arithmetic properties of a 1-form's periods determine global solvability and hypoellipticity in differential complexes, extending prior work on this topic.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the differential complex associated with a closed, real, non-singular 1-form, examining global solvability at the first and last levels, and in the rational case, for all degrees. Global hypoellipticity is characterized by building on Meziani's work.
AI.result: Complete characterization of global solvability in the first and last levels; for rational 1-forms, global solvability for all degrees and a full description of cohomology spaces; complete characterization of global hypoellipticity.
AI.conclusion: All conditions for global solvability and hypoellipticity depend solely on the arithmetic nature of the form's periods.

title: Graph-Based ECG Synthesis with Activation-Consistency Certification and Diagnostics-Aware Morphology Curation
summary: Synthetic electrocardiogram (ECG) generation can support algorithm development and robustness evaluation, but simulated signals must preserve interpretable activation, recovery, and morphology properties. We present a graph-based ECG synthesis framework that combines activation-consistency certification with diagnostics-aware morphology curation. A unified heart graph supports an eikonal-template backend (ET) and a pseudo-diffusion reaction--eikonal backend (RE). We formulate graph Eikonal activation as a Bellman fixed-point problem and use the Bellman residual as a computable certificate for activation-time consistency. Each simulated ECG is evaluated by a two-stage diagnostics pipeline that separates metric computation from experiment-specific acceptance policies. On the cardiac graph, RE-derived activation times showed near-millisecond agreement with the Eikonal backbone and achieved $R^2=0.99876$ after causal predecessor filtering. Recovery experiments showed that endo-epicardial APD gradients determined the main T-wave morphology window, whereas the diffusion strength $κ$ provided secondary repolarization smoothing. In final balanced multi-lead curation, RE accepted 658/2000 samples versus 578/2000 for ET and increased per-model morphology coverage from 0.09248 to 0.09888. The framework provides a conservative basis for controllable and curated synthetic ECG generation.

AI.tldr: A graph-based ECG synthesis framework with activation-consistency certification and diagnostics-aware morphology curation, using Bellman residual for verifiable activation timing and two-stage diagnostics for quality control.
AI.motivation: To generate synthetic ECG signals that preserve interpretable cardiac properties (activation, recovery, morphology) while enabling controllable and verifiable synthesis for algorithm development and robustness evaluation.
AI.method: A unified heart graph supporting two backends (Eikonal-template ET and pseudo-diffusion reaction–eikonal RE). Graph Eikonal activation is formulated as a Bellman fixed-point problem with Bellman residual as a computable certificate. A two-stage diagnostics pipeline separates metric computation from acceptance policies.
AI.result: RE-derived activation times achieved near-millisecond agreement with Eikonal backbone (R²=0.99876). Endo-epicardial APD gradients determined T-wave morphology; diffusion strength κ provided secondary smoothing. RE accepted 658/2000 samples vs 578/2000 for ET, with morphology coverage increasing from 0.09248 to 0.09888.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a conservative, controllable, and curated basis for synthetic ECG generation with verifiable consistency and improved morphological coverage.

title: Real Quantum Chemistry With Complex Orbitals
summary: We follow up our study of basis set truncation errors for atoms in magnetic fields [Åström and Lehtola, J. Phys. Chem. A, 2023, 127, 10872]. Our previous study employed an approximate real-valued model. In this work, we implement a scheme to allow the use of complex basis functions and the true, complex Hamiltonian with linear molecules in a parallel magnetic field within the usual real-basis machinery of quantum chemistry. Our method performs additional unitary transformations before and after a conventional Fock build, thus allowing the reuse of existing software methods and algorithms. We apply our approach to calculations on low-lying configurations of the atoms $Z \leq 18$ in static magnetic fields up to 0.6 $B_0$. The calculations employ the uncontracted aug-cc-pVTZ and the benchmarking quality AHGBSP3-9 Gaussian-type orbital basis sets. We compare total energies obtained with real and complex orbitals using these basis sets to fully numerical ones at the complete basis set limit. We see that the states of the real-valued Hamiltonian are superpositions of the true eigenstates that are correctly captured by the complex calculations. Our results show that the complex basis machinery is necessary for targeting states with the correct symmetry for the studied range of magnetic field strengths. The novel tool is key for future work where we aim to optimize basis sets for finite-field calculations.

AI.tldr: This paper extends prior work on basis set truncation errors for atoms in magnetic fields by implementing a complex basis function scheme that works with the true complex Hamiltonian, showing that complex calculations are necessary for correct symmetry targeting in magnetic fields up to 0.6 B₀ for atoms Z ≤ 18.
AI.motivation: Previous work used an approximate real-valued model; this study aims to implement and validate a scheme using complex basis functions with the true complex Hamiltonian for atoms in magnetic fields.
AI.method: The method performs additional unitary transformations before and after a conventional Fock build, enabling reuse of existing real-basis quantum chemistry machinery for complex calculations.
AI.result: Complex calculations correctly capture superpositions of true eigenstates that real-valued Hamiltonian cannot; complex basis machinery is necessary for targeting correct symmetry in the studied magnetic field range.
AI.conclusion: The complex basis tool is essential for future work on optimizing basis sets for finite-field calculations.

title: Integrating Gyrokinetic Flux Predictions with Ideal MHD Stability Boundaries
summary: Accurate prediction of pedestal height and width in tokamaks remains a critical issue as it strongly influences the predicted plasma performance of all future reactors. We present an integrated pedestal-stability workflow that combines equilibrium scans with magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) stability analysis using ELITE and GATO and gyrokinetic transport predictions using CGYRO/QLGYRO. The workflow reproduces the characteristic KBM first- and second-stability structure previously identified in gyrokinetic pedestal studies. Applied to spherical tokamaks (STs), the workflow shows good agreement with past studies when low-n peeling stability is included, emphasizing the importance of resolving low-n physics in ST pedestals. Extending the analysis to SPARC-like, high toroidal field plasmas, kinetic ballooning mode (KBM) and microtearing mode (MTM) heat fluxes are found to increase strongly with toroidal field, suggesting that access to the KBM second-stability region may become significantly more difficult in high toroidal field devices. Comparison with global ELITE finite-n analysis at SPARC parameters suggests that the H-mode pedestal lies in an intermediate regime bounded by local KBM second stability on one side and global finite-n ballooning instability on the other, consistent with the EPED picture once global effects are included

AI.tldr: This paper presents an integrated pedestal-stability workflow combining MHD stability analysis (ELITE/GATO) and gyrokinetic transport predictions (CGYRO/QLGYRO) to predict pedestal height and width in tokamaks. Applied to spherical tokamaks and SPARC-like high toroidal field plasmas, the work shows that KBM and MTM heat fluxes increase strongly with toroidal field, making second-stability access more difficult in high-field devices, and suggests the H-mode pedestal lies in an intermediate regime bounded by local KBM second stability and global finite-n ballooning instability.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of pedestal height and width in tokamaks is critical for predicting plasma performance in future reactors, but remains a challenging issue.
AI.method: An integrated pedestal-stability workflow combining equilibrium scans with MHD stability analysis (ELITE, GATO) and gyrokinetic transport predictions (CGYRO/QLGYRO).
AI.result: The workflow reproduces the KBM first- and second-stability structure. For STs, low-n peeling stability is important. For high-field plasmas (SPARC-like), KBM and MTM heat fluxes increase strongly with toroidal field, making second-stability access more difficult.
AI.conclusion: The H-mode pedestal in high-field devices lies in an intermediate regime bounded by local KBM second stability and global finite-n ballooning instability, consistent with the EPED picture when global effects are included.

title: Regularity of global attractors for beam equations with fractional damping and memory
summary: This paper investigates the long-time behavior of a semilinear beam equation in a domain $Ω\subset R^{n}$, with memory and fractional damping of the form $(-Δ)^αu_{t}$ ($α\in [0,2]$ the dissipation index). Two critical growth indices of the nonlinear term are determined for smooth and $C^2$ boundaries respectively, concerning the existence of the associated semigroup. We prove the existence of global attractor for the semigroup by showing that it possesses a bounded absorbing set and asymptotic compactness. Furthermore, we find out a new way to obtain, for all $α$, higher regularities than anticipated for the attractors, and the regularity result indicates an interesting phenomenon that even much weaker damping can produce regularity that is infinitely close to that in the case of strong damping ($α=2$). As a consequence, our regularity result deepens and extends the existing related ones for the case when the memory is absent.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a semilinear beam equation with memory and fractional damping, proving existence of global attractors and achieving higher regularity than expected, showing that even weak damping can yield near-strong-damping regularity.
AI.motivation: To understand the long-time behavior of beam equations with memory and fractional damping, and to determine critical growth indices for nonlinear terms on different boundary types.
AI.method: The authors analyze critical growth indices for smooth and C² boundaries, prove existence of a bounded absorbing set and asymptotic compactness, and develop a novel approach to obtain higher regularities for attractors across all dissipation indices α.
AI.result: Global attractor existence is proven; higher regularities than anticipated are achieved for all α, with the surprising result that even much weaker damping produces regularity infinitely close to the strong damping (α=2) case.
AI.conclusion: The regularity results deepen and extend existing results for the case without memory, revealing an interesting phenomenon about the relationship between damping strength and attractor regularity.

title: Adaptive Randomized Pivoting for Tensor Singular Value Decomposition Model
summary: This paper studies how adaptive randomized pivoting (ARP), recently introduced for matrix column subset selection, can be extended to tensors in the t-product framework. We propose two constructions. The first one, called ARP-T-CUR, applies matrix ARP independently to the frontal slices of the tensor in the Fourier domain. This gives a Fourier-slicewise CUR approximation and leads to a direct expected-error bound inherited from the matrix theory. The second construction, called T-ARP, selects common lateral and horizontal slices for the whole tensor. This produces a genuine tensor cross approximation in the t-product sense, but also introduces a new difficulty: the same pivot indices must be used across all Fourier slices. We make this coupling explicit and prove an expected-error bound under a frequency-alignment condition measuring how far the common tensor-level sampling rule is from the slice-wise ARP sampling rules. This condition recovers the usual $r+1$-type factor when the leverage-score distributions are aligned across frequencies. We also discuss the resulting tensor cross approximation and its connection with t-DEIM. Numerical experiments on synthetic tensors, images, and videos illustrate the behavior of the proposed methods and show the benefit of common-index tensor sampling over standard tensor cross baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper extends adaptive randomized pivoting (ARP) to tensor column subset selection within the t-product framework, proposing two methods: ARP-T-CUR (independent Fourier slice-wise CUR) and T-ARP (common-index tensor cross approximation), with theoretical error bounds and numerical experiments on synthetic data, images, and videos.
AI.motivation: To extend the recently introduced adaptive randomized pivoting (ARP) method for matrix column subset selection to tensors under the t-product framework, enabling efficient tensor cross approximation with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Two constructions are proposed: (1) ARP-T-CUR applies matrix ARP independently to each frontal slice of the tensor in the Fourier domain; (2) T-ARP selects common lateral and horizontal slices across all Fourier slices, enforcing a coupling that is analyzed via a frequency-alignment condition.
AI.result: Expected-error bounds are proved for both methods; the frequency-alignment condition recovers the usual r+1-type factor when leverage-score distributions are aligned; numerical experiments on synthetic tensors, images, and videos demonstrate the benefit of common-index tensor sampling over standard baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed T-ARP method yields a genuine t-product tensor cross approximation with theoretical guarantees, and common-index sampling proves advantageous over slice-wise independent sampling in practice.

title: Challenging the $p$-type Paradigm: Intrinsic $n$-type Mobility in Antiferromagnetic Cr$_2$O$_3$
summary: Chromium oxide (Cr$_2$O$_3$) is widely considered a $p$-type transparent conducting oxide despite ongoing debate regarding its intrinsic transport character. Here, we resolve this question by computing phonon-limited electron and hole mobilities using the ab initio Boltzmann transport equation. We find that electron mobility systematically exceeds hole mobility over a wide temperature range, demonstrating that Cr$_2$O$_3$ is intrinsically $n$-type. Analysis of scattering mechanisms reveals that scattering with phonons affects electrons and holes similarly, and that the mobility asymmetry originates from the electronic structure, namely the larger effective mass and multi-valley character of the valence band. The intrinsic $n$-type character, combined with moderate hole mobility, enables bipolar transport and revises the role of Cr$_2$O$_3$ in transparent electronics. Additionally, our results on mobility complement previous studies on defect formation indicating that the commonly observed $p$-type behavior is extrinsic. These insights provide a complete chemical-transport paradigm for Cr$_2$O$_3$, re-evaluating its role in functional transparent electronic and magneto-optoelectronic applications

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the debate about whether Cr₂O₃ is intrinsically p-type or n-type by computing phonon-limited charge mobilities, finding it is intrinsically n-type due to electronic structure differences (effective mass and multi-valley valence band), while observed p-type behavior is extrinsic.
AI.motivation: To resolve the ongoing debate regarding the intrinsic transport character of Cr₂O₃, which is widely considered p-type despite lacking definitive evidence for its intrinsic behavior.
AI.method: Ab initio Boltzmann transport equation calculations of phonon-limited electron and hole mobilities in Cr₂O₃.
AI.result: Electron mobility systematically exceeds hole mobility over a wide temperature range, proving intrinsic n-type character. The mobility asymmetry stems from electronic structure (larger effective mass and multi-valley valence band), not differential phonon scattering.
AI.conclusion: Cr₂O₃ is intrinsically n-type with moderate hole mobility enabling bipolar transport; observed p-type behavior is extrinsic, re-evaluating its role in transparent electronics and magneto-optoelectronic applications.

title: The Negative Triangularity Tokamak Path for Fusion Pilot Plants: Experimental Progress and Future Prospects
comment: 23 pages, 28 pages, based on talk at 2025 APS DPP meeting
summary: This paper reviews the experimental progress of negative triangularity (NT), a tokamak configuration where the poloidal cross-section is a reversed-D shape compared to the conventional positive triangularity (PT) shape. NT is a promising reactor scenario that addresses the fundamental tension between performance, exhaust, and robustness. NT studies have accelerated globally across these three pillars over the past several years. While tokamak pilot plants are typically designed for the standard PT H-mode regime, this approach faces significant challenges in balancing high core performance with manageable heat and particle exhaust as well as reliable robustness. In contrast, NT plasmas have achieved H-mode-level confinement while remaining robustly free of the deleterious edge localized mode (ELM) instability. Regarding exhaust, NT offers a larger divertor wetted area on the outboard side and demonstrates compatibility with detachment and operation at high core radiation fraction without the constraints of the L-H power threshold, while also exhibiting low core impurity retention. NT operates with high reproducibility over a wide operating space, demonstrated by robust discharge-to-discharge consistency, and has access to plasmas with very high Greenwald fractions and/or low edge safety factors compared to PT H-mode plasmas. Further research is required to answer outstanding questions related to reactor confinement extrapolation, the optimal triangularity for a reactor, and core-edge integration. NT studies in existing and planned tokamaks are increasing, as is interest in possible reactor concepts. The unique physics and engineering advantages of NT offer a robust and simplified foundation for a viable fusion power plant.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews negative triangularity (NT) tokamak configuration as a promising reactor scenario that balances core performance, exhaust management, and operational robustness better than conventional positive triangularity (PT) H-mode, while remaining ELM-free and offering larger divertor wetted area, detachment compatibility, high core radiation fraction, and high reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Tokamak pilot plants based on standard PT H-mode face fundamental tension between high core performance, manageable heat/particle exhaust, and reliable robustness. NT configuration is explored as an alternative to resolve this trade-off.
AI.method: The paper reviews global experimental progress of NT plasmas across three pillars: core performance (H-mode-level confinement), exhaust (larger divertor wetted area, detachment compatibility, high core radiation fraction), and robustness (ELM-free, high reproducibility, high Greenwald fractions, low edge safety factor operation).
AI.result: NT plasmas achieve H-mode-level confinement without ELMs, offer larger outboard divertor wetted area, demonstrate detachment compatibility, operate at high core radiation fraction without L-H threshold constraints, exhibit low core impurity retention, and show high discharge-to-discharge reproducibility across a wide operating space.
AI.conclusion: NT offers unique physics and engineering advantages that provide a robust and simplified foundation for a viable fusion power plant, though further research is needed on reactor confinement extrapolation, optimal triangularity, and core-edge integration.

title: Qualitative analysis of positive singular solutions for a critical elliptic system in a punctured ball
summary: We study qualitative properties of positive singular solutions to a weakly coupled elliptic system with critical exponents in a punctured ball. We give a sharp criterion on the removability of the isolated singularity. We also prove the nonexistence of positive solutions with one component bounded near the singularity and the other component unbounded near the singularity. Asymptotic symmetry and sharp pointwise estimates are also proved for singular solutions. These generalizes some classical results of (Caffarelli, Gidas and Spruck, Comm. Pure Appl. Math, 1989) to the weakly coupled system.

AI.tldr: This paper studies positive singular solutions to a weakly coupled elliptic system with critical exponents, establishing criteria for singularity removability, proving nonexistence of mixed bounded/unbounded component solutions, and deriving asymptotic symmetry and pointwise estimates, generalizing classical results to systems.
AI.motivation: To extend classical results on singular solutions (Caffarelli, Gidas, Spruck, 1989) from scalar elliptic equations to weakly coupled elliptic systems with critical exponents.
AI.method: Qualitative analysis of positive singular solutions in a punctured ball using weakly coupled elliptic system theory with critical exponents.
AI.result: (1) A sharp criterion for removability of isolated singularities; (2) Nonexistence of positive solutions where one component is bounded and the other unbounded near the singularity; (3) Asymptotic symmetry and sharp pointwise estimates for singular solutions.
AI.conclusion: The classical results for single elliptic equations are successfully generalized to weakly coupled systems, providing new insights into singularity behavior in coupled settings.

title: TVD and TVB preservation without TVD time discretization for discontinuous Galerkin methods
summary: Total variation diminishing (TVD) and total variation bounded (TVB) properties are crucial for controlling spurious oscillations in numerical solutions of conservation laws. In the classical Runge--Kutta (RK) discontinuous Galerkin (DG) framework, enforcing these properties is intrinsically tied to TVD time integrators, more commonly known today as strong-stability-preserving (SSP) methods. This reliance imposes severe structural restrictions, including order barriers and incompatibility with various fully discrete DG formulations, ranging from the recent RKDG method with compact stencils (cRKDG) to the widely established Arbitrary DERivative (ADER) DG method. To bypass these constraints, we propose a novel trace-limited corrector framework that preserves the TVD/TVB-in-the-means properties using generic, non-SSP time stepping. Based on Harten's lemma, our key insight is that total variation stability is dictated solely by the cell-average update in the final corrector stage. Consequently, we modify the traces in the numerical fluxes exclusively in the final stage, leaving the intermediate predictor stages unconstrained. This strategy decouples oscillation control from the SSP restriction, accommodates standard RKDG, cRKDG, and ADER-DG predictors, and retains the compactness of the cRKDG framework. We also prove that the limiter does not activate in smooth regions, thereby preserving the underlying accuracy. Finally, numerical experiments are presented to demonstrate the capabilities and robustness of the method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a trace-limited corrector framework that preserves TVD/TVB-in-the-means properties for DG methods using generic non-SSP time stepping, bypassing the restrictive SSP requirement by modifying traces only in the final corrector stage based on Harten's lemma.
AI.motivation: Classical RKDG frameworks enforce TVD/TVB properties through SSP time integrators, which imposes severe structural restrictions like order barriers and incompatibility with modern DG formulations such as cRKDG and ADER-DG.
AI.method: A trace-limited corrector framework that modifies numerical flux traces exclusively in the final corrector stage based on Harten's lemma, while leaving intermediate predictor stages unconstrained, thus decoupling oscillation control from SSP requirements.
AI.result: The method accommodates standard RKDG, cRKDG, and ADER-DG predictors, retains compactness of cRKDG framework, and the limiter does not activate in smooth regions, preserving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully bypasses SSP restrictions while maintaining TVD/TVB-in-the-means properties, supported by numerical experiments demonstrating robustness and capability.

title: Topological phase transition driven by in-plane spin rotation
summary: The intrinsic coupling between magnetism and nontrivial band topology in magnetic topological insulators makes external magnetic fields a powerful tool for manipulating topological states. However, conventional magnetic control mechanisms, such as driving magnetic phase transitions or fully reversing magnetization, typically demand large magnetic fields and lack continuous tunability. Here, we establish a symmetry framework for the reversible switching of topological states via continuous in-plane spin rotation, governed by magnetic point group constraints on the Berry curvature distribution. Using a two-dimensional kagome ferromagnetic Chern insulator as a prototype, we demonstrate that a 60°in-plane magnetization rotation reverses the sign of the Chern number, transitioning through a topologically trivial state. Crucially, micromagnetic simulations confirm that this spin-reorientation-driven switching operates under exceptionally small magnetic fields and on ultrafast timescales. This work provides a highly efficient, low-energy paradigm for the manipulation of topological states.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that a 60° in-plane magnetization rotation can reversibly switch the Chern number sign in a kagome ferromagnetic Chern insulator, enabled by symmetry constraints on Berry curvature. The switching requires ultra-small magnetic fields and operates at ultrafast timescales, offering a low-energy paradigm for topological state manipulation.
AI.motivation: Conventional magnetic control of topological states (e.g., driving phase transitions or full magnetization reversal) requires large magnetic fields and lacks continuous tunability. The authors seek a more efficient, low-energy approach.
AI.method: The authors establish a symmetry framework linking magnetic point group constraints to Berry curvature distribution. They use a 2D kagome ferromagnetic Chern insulator as a prototype and perform micromagnetic simulations to validate the spin-reorientation-driven switching mechanism.
AI.result: A 60° in-plane magnetization rotation reverses the Chern number sign, passing through a topologically trivial state. This switching is achievable under exceptionally small magnetic fields and on ultrafast timescales.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a highly efficient, low-energy paradigm for manipulating topological states via continuous in-plane spin rotation, overcoming the limitations of conventional magnetic control methods.

title: Identification of MHD equilibrium $β$ limits for CFQS plasmas
summary: The magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) equilibrium $β$ limits in the Chinese First QuasiAxisymmetric Stellarator (CFQS) are investigated using the NTEC code, for both the standard and the magnetic island configurations. The equilibrium $β$ limit is identified upon the onset of the rapid destruction of nested flux surfaces by evaluating several numerical metrics, including the fractal dimension, weighted Birkhoff average, and effective volume of parallel diffusion. In the standard configuration, the net-current-free and the bootstrap-current-carrying equilibria can sustain well-ordered magnetic surfaces up to $\langleβ\rangle\approx1.5\%$. The proliferation of stochastic field lines starts after the critical overlap between the internal major islands and the high-order island chains. Two types of divertor island configurations are studied based on net-current-free equilibria. It is found that the edge islands may transition into open field lines at low $\langleβ\rangle$ values and lead to a drastic shrinkage of the last closed flux surface. Meanwhile, the threshold $\langleβ\rangle$ value of the degradation of inner flux surfaces is similar to the standard configuration.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates MHD equilibrium β limits in the CFQS stellarator using the NTEC code, finding that nested flux surfaces can sustain up to ⟨β⟩≈1.5% in standard configurations, while edge islands in divertor configurations cause earlier degradation.
AI.motivation: To determine the MHD equilibrium β limits in the CFQS stellarator, which is crucial for assessing its performance and stability as a fusion device.
AI.method: The NTEC code is used to simulate equilibria; the onset of flux surface destruction is identified via fractal dimension, weighted Birkhoff average, and effective volume of parallel diffusion.
AI.result: Net-current-free and bootstrap-current-carrying equilibria sustain well-ordered magnetic surfaces up to ⟨β⟩≈1.5%. Edge islands in divertor configurations transition to open field lines at low ⟨β⟩, shrinking the last closed flux surface, while inner flux surface thresholds remain similar.
AI.conclusion: The CFQS standard configuration has a β limit around 1.5%, limited by island overlap. Divertor island configurations degrade edge confinement at lower β values.

title: The Calderón problem for near-Euclidean metrics
summary: In this article, we consider the anisotropic Calderón problem of determining the potential from boundary measurements of the time-independent Schrödinger equation on compact Riemannian manifolds with boundary. We prove a global uniqueness theorem for small perturbations of the Euclidean metric.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global uniqueness for the anisotropic Calderón problem with small metric perturbations on compact Riemannian manifolds.
AI.motivation: To determine the potential in the Schrödinger equation from boundary measurements on manifolds with anisotropic metrics, building on the classical Calderón problem.
AI.method: The study considers time-independent Schrödinger equations on compact Riemannian manifolds with boundary, focusing on small perturbations of the Euclidean metric to prove global uniqueness.
AI.result: A global uniqueness theorem is established for small perturbations of the Euclidean metric in the anisotropic Calderón problem.
AI.conclusion: The anisotropic Calderón problem admits a unique solution under small metric perturbations, extending understanding of inverse problems on curved domains.

title: Regularization of the metric generalized inverse in Banach spaces and the dichotomy phenomenon
summary: For a bounded linear operator acting between Banach spaces, its metric generalized inverse is the analog to the prominent Moore-Penrose inverse for operators acting between Hilbert spaces. This generalized inverse is well-defined for Banach spaces that are strictly convex and reflexive. Previous studies had been restricted to closed range situations, where the metric generalized inverse constitutes a continuous homogeneous mapping. The focus of the present study is on the ill-posed situation in the sense of Nashed, when the governing operator has a non-closed range. We define and analyze iterative schemes as the Landweber and the Schulz-Newton method, as well as parametric schemes with focus on a specific Tikhonov method. Both types are called regularizations aimed at approximating the metric generalized inverse. As a fundamental feature of such schemes we observe a dichotomy. This emphasizes that these schemes, when applied to elements of the domain of the metric generalized inverse, approximate the corresponding best approximate solutions well, whereas the resulting approximations will be asymptotically unbounded if they are applied to elements that do not belong to the domain of the metric generalized inverse.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the study of metric generalized inverses for bounded linear operators in Banach spaces from closed-range to ill-posed (non-closed range) situations, analyzing iterative (Landweber, Schulz-Newton) and parametric (Tikhonov) regularization schemes, and revealing a dichotomy where approximations work well within the inverse's domain but become asymptotically unbounded outside it.
AI.motivation: Previous studies of metric generalized inverses in Banach spaces were restricted to closed range situations. The authors aim to address the ill-posed case (non-closed range), which is more challenging and practically relevant, by developing and analyzing regularization schemes for approximating the metric generalized inverse in this setting.
AI.method: The paper defines and analyzes iterative methods (Landweber method, Schulz-Newton method) and parametric schemes (Tikhonov regularization) as regularizations to approximate the metric generalized inverse for operators with non-closed range in strictly convex and reflexive Banach spaces.
AI.result: A fundamental dichotomy is identified: when applied to elements within the domain of the metric generalized inverse, the schemes approximate best approximate solutions well; when applied to elements outside the domain, the approximations become asymptotically unbounded.
AI.conclusion: Regularization schemes for metric generalized inverses in Banach spaces exhibit a clear dichotomy based on domain membership, providing both a theoretical foundation and practical limitations for handling ill-posed inverse problems in Banach space settings.

title: CHESS: CHEbyshev pSeudo-Spectral transport for Feynman integral differential equations
summary: We present CHESS (CHEbyshev pSeudo Spectrum), a Wolfram Language package for high-precision one-dimensional transport of ε-factorized differential equations for Feynman master integrals. The solver works with the matrix obtained by pulling a differential one-form to a chosen path. This matrix may be supplied directly, or assembled from constant matrices and precomputed scalar pullbacks of the one-forms. The program combines Chebyshev-Lobatto spectral collocation, sparse matrix assembly, sequential propagation in the ε-expansion, and residue-based regularization of spurious regular singular endpoints. Benchmarks for large multi-scale integral families show rapid node convergence and agreement with independent reference data where such data are available. In the fixed local-series comparison used here, the Chebyshev transports also give shorter wall times; the reported process-tree memory usage is comparable for the smaller parallel runs and lower for the largest benchmark system in that comparison.

AI.tldr: CHESS is a Wolfram Language package that uses Chebyshev-Lobatto spectral collocation methods for high-precision numerical evaluation of Feynman master integrals via ε-factorized differential equations, demonstrating rapid convergence and competitive performance.
AI.motivation: To provide a high-precision numerical method for solving ε-factorized differential equations for Feynman master integrals, which are essential for multi-loop calculations in particle physics.
AI.method: The solver combines Chebyshev-Lobatto spectral collocation, sparse matrix assembly, sequential propagation in ε-expansion, and residue-based regularization of spurious regular singular endpoints. It works with matrices obtained by pulling differential one-forms to chosen paths.
AI.result: The package shows rapid node convergence for large multi-scale integral families, agreement with independent reference data, and shorter wall times compared to fixed local-series methods, with comparable or lower memory usage.
AI.conclusion: CHESS provides an efficient, high-precision tool for computing Feynman master integrals, outperforming traditional local-series methods in speed while maintaining accuracy and manageable memory consumption.

title: A possible approach to overcome the saturation of the neutron yield in a Plasma Focus and to achieve breakeven
summary: Saturation of the neutron yield with increasing energy of the condenser bank in a Plasma Focus led to the shutdown of PF research focussed on controlled nuclear fusion in the past. We review available models of saturation and develop further the model of Lee S., Applied Phys. Lett. 95, 151503, 2009. This model relies on the well-known and generally accepted model of Lee S., J. Fusion Energy 2014, 33, 319 of Plasma Focus discharges and describes saturation in terms of the dynamic resistance, i.e. the rate of change of PF inductance due to the motion of the plasma sheath during rundown. A model of this sheath discussed in Di Vita A., J. Plasma Physics, 1993, 50, 1 shows that its spontaneous filamentation rules the dynamic resistance, spoiling the power supply from the condenser bank to the plasma at the values of condenser bank energy above 0.5 MJ values which are relevant to a fusion reactor. Together, these two models lead to the conclusion that suppression of such filamentation prevents saturation, multiplies the PF drive parameter by a factor 3 at least and allows breakeven in a 224 kV, 10 MJ Plasma Focus working with DT. We can suppress filamentation by superimposing a radial magnetic field to the interelectrode region of the Plasma Focus where rundown occurs. A conservative estimate shows that a 1.4 T radial magnetic field is enough to suppress many known filamentation instabilities. Suitably located magnets can generate this field. Their layout resembles the layout of the sources of radial magnetic field in the cylindrical geometry of a Hall thruster for space propulsion. For high temperature superconducting magnets, the required current density is too small to trigger quenching.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the saturation of neutron yield in Plasma Focus devices at high condenser bank energies, proposing that plasma sheath filamentation causes dynamic resistance that limits energy transfer. The authors combine existing models to show that suppressing filamentation with a radial magnetic field could enable fusion breakeven in a 224 kV, 10 MJ DT Plasma Focus.
AI.motivation: To understand and overcome the historical neutron yield saturation problem that led to the shutdown of Plasma Focus research for controlled nuclear fusion, and to find a feasible path toward fusion breakeven.
AI.method: Reviews and extends the Lee model (2009) of neutron yield saturation, combining it with Di Vita's filamentation model (1993) to link dynamic resistance to plasma sheath filamentation. Proposes suppressing filamentation by superimposing a radial magnetic field (estimated ~1.4 T) in the interelectrode region, inspired by Hall thruster magnetic field layouts.
AI.result: The combined models predict that suppressing filamentation could multiply the Plasma Focus drive parameter by at least 3×, enabling breakeven in a 224 kV, 10 MJ DT device. A 1.4 T radial magnetic field is estimated sufficient to suppress known filamentation instabilities. High-temperature superconducting magnets can generate this field without quenching.
AI.conclusion: Filamentation-induced dynamic resistance is the root cause of neutron yield saturation. Suppressing it via radial magnetic fields can unlock higher performance and potentially achieve fusion breakeven in Plasma Focus devices.

title: Sharp endpoint multilinear estimates for oscillatory integrals and spectral clusters
summary: We prove sharp $k$-linear $L^p$ estimates for Carleson--Sjölin oscillatory integral operators with arbitrary separated frequency scales for all $k\ge 2$ and $1\le p\le \infty$. The estimates are sharp, including the endpoint logarithmic behavior for general Carleson--Sjölin phases. Moreover, we obtain log-free endpoint bilinear spectral cluster estimates on every closed three-dimensional Riemannian manifold, resolving a problem of Burq--Gérard--Tzvetkov. As a consequence, we establish sharp $k$-linear $L^p$ spectral cluster estimates for all $k\ge 2$ and $1\le p\le \infty$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves sharp k-linear Lp estimates for Carleson-Sjölin oscillatory integral operators with arbitrary separated frequency scales for all k≥2 and 1≤p≤∞, including endpoint logarithmic behavior. It also obtains log-free endpoint bilinear spectral cluster estimates on every closed 3D Riemannian manifold, resolving a problem of Burq-Gérard-Tzvetkov, and establishes sharp k-linear Lp spectral cluster estimates.
AI.motivation: To establish sharp multilinear estimates for oscillatory integral operators and spectral clusters, resolving open problems including the Burq-Gérard-Tzvetkov conjecture on 3D manifolds.
AI.method: The paper uses techniques from harmonic analysis and Fourier integral operator theory to prove sharp k-linear Lp estimates for Carleson-Sjölin operators with separated frequency scales, and applies these to obtain log-free bilinear spectral cluster estimates on closed 3D Riemannian manifolds.
AI.result: Sharp k-linear Lp estimates for Carleson-Sjölin operators for all k≥2 and 1≤p≤∞, including endpoint logarithmic behavior. Log-free endpoint bilinear spectral cluster estimates on every closed 3D Riemannian manifold.
AI.conclusion: The paper resolves a problem of Burq-Gérard-Tzvetkov and establishes sharp multilinear spectral cluster estimates, with the results being optimal including at the endpoint.

title: Recycling singular and projection subspaces for pseudospectra computation
comment: 30 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables; supplementary material included
summary: Computing matrix pseudospectra over a prescribed region requires evaluating the smallest singular value of $C-zI$ at a large number of grid points, which can be prohibitively expensive for large-scale matrices. We develop a recycling-based framework for accelerating such computations for both dense and sparse matrices. The main idea is to exploit the correlation between singular value problems at neighboring grid points by adaptively recycling singular subspaces computed at previously visited points by an iterative SVD solver. We develop fast Rayleigh-Ritz-SVD procedures for extracting Ritz singular pairs from the recycled singular subspaces, together with fast residual evaluation procedures, with an overall cost that scales linearly with the number of recycled samples. When the iterative SVD solver admits preconditioning, we propose using a two-level preconditioner whose projection subspaces are recycled. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed recycling strategies yield substantial speedups over existing methods while maintaining the accuracy of the computed pseudospectra.

AI.tldr: A recycling-based framework accelerates matrix pseudospectra computation by reusing singular subspaces from neighboring grid points, reducing computational cost while maintaining accuracy for both dense and sparse matrices.
AI.motivation: Computing matrix pseudospectra over a region requires evaluating the smallest singular value at many grid points, which is prohibitively expensive for large-scale matrices using standard methods.
AI.method: Exploits correlation between singular value problems at neighboring grid points by adaptively recycling singular subspaces via fast Rayleigh-Ritz-SVD procedures with linear scaling cost; also proposes two-level preconditioner recycling for iterative SVD solvers.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show substantial speedups over existing methods while maintaining accuracy of computed pseudospectra.
AI.conclusion: Recycling singular subspaces across grid points is an effective acceleration strategy for pseudospectra computation, applicable to both dense and sparse large-scale matrices.

title: Influence of Park's Two-Temperature Model Control Temperature on the Flow Properties in Hypersonic Reentry Conditions
comment: 24 pages, 18 figures, and 4 tables, part of the proceedings of the AIAA Aviation Forum and ASCEND 2025
summary: Numerical simulations of reactive hypersonic flows under thermochemical non-equilibrium conditions are presented for the FIRE II and Mars Pathfinder capsules. An 11-species chemical model is employed to simulate Earth's atmosphere, while an 8-species chemical model simulates Mars' atmosphere. The current formulation uses Park's two-temperature model to account for the non-equilibrium phenomena. The present work analyzes the impact of different sets of weight factors used in Park's model to calculate the control temperature. The code used to simulate the hypersonic flow addressed in this work solves the Navier-Stokes equations for reacting gas flows. The findings are depicted in terms of the Mach number, temperature modes, and mass fraction distributions along the stagnation streamline in a region closer to the shock wave. The study also includes results regarding the stagnation point convective heat flux. The results presented are encouraging and show that the weight factors significantly impact the FIRE II test cases while having little impact on the Mars Pathfinder flows. In all cases, it is possible to observe some effect of the weight factor selection on property distributions. In summary, the weight factors influence the flow behavior with varying intensities depending on the flow conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how different weight factors in Park's two-temperature model affect numerical simulations of reactive hypersonic flows for FIRE II (Earth) and Mars Pathfinder capsules, finding significant impact on Earth entry cases but little effect on Mars entry flows.
AI.motivation: To understand the influence of weight factor selection in Park's two-temperature model on predicting thermochemical non-equilibrium hypersonic flows for planetary entry capsules.
AI.method: Numerical simulations solving Navier-Stokes equations for reacting gas flows using Park's two-temperature model with different weight factor sets, applied to FIRE II (11-species Earth atmosphere) and Mars Pathfinder (8-species Mars atmosphere) cases.
AI.result: Weight factors significantly impact FIRE II test cases (Earth entry) but have little impact on Mars Pathfinder flows. Some influence on property distributions observed in all cases.
AI.conclusion: Weight factors influence flow behavior with varying intensities depending on flow conditions, affecting simulation accuracy for different planetary entry scenarios.

title: Ion firehose and ion cyclotron instability with subtracted-Kappa distributions
summary: In the present paper we discuss numerical solutions of the dispersion relation for electromagnetic waves propagating along the lines of an ambient magnetic field, considering parameters representative of space plasma environments, and considering the ion population described by a subtracted-Kappa distribution. We consider situations in which the ion thermal anisotropy is such that $T_{i\perp}<T_{i\parallel}$, and investigate the effect of some parameters associated to the subtracted-Kappa distribution on the ion firehose instability. We also consider situations in which $T_{i\perp}>T_{i\parallel}$, and investigate the effects of the same parameters on the ion-cyclotron instability. For both forms of instability, we also investigate the effect of the thermal anisotropy of the electron distribution, and the effect of the occurrence of a drift velocity in the electron distribution function. Among the results obtained, we show that the increase of the loss-cone feature in a bi-kappa distribution leads to a decrease of the growth rates of the firehose instability, and to an increase of the growth rates of the ion-cyclotron instability.

AI.tldr: This paper numerically analyzes the dispersion relation for electromagnetic waves in space plasma, focusing on ion firehose and ion-cyclotron instabilities using a subtracted-Kappa ion distribution. It examines the effects of ion thermal anisotropy, electron anisotropy, and electron drift, finding that stronger loss-cone features reduce firehose growth rates but increase ion-cyclotron growth rates.
AI.motivation: To understand how parameters of the subtracted-Kappa distribution (a model for non-thermal space plasmas) affect ion firehose and ion-cyclotron instabilities, which are important for wave-particle interactions and plasma dynamics in space environments.
AI.method: Numerical solution of the dispersion relation for electromagnetic waves propagating parallel to the ambient magnetic field, using a subtracted-Kappa distribution for ions and considering thermal anisotropy and electron drift effects.
AI.result: Increasing the loss-cone feature in a bi-kappa distribution decreases growth rates of the firehose instability but increases growth rates of the ion-cyclotron instability.
AI.conclusion: The subtracted-Kappa distribution parameters significantly influence both firehose and ion-cyclotron instabilities, with opposite effects on growth rates, highlighting the importance of non-thermal distribution features in space plasma wave dynamics.

title: Sharp Lifespan Estimates and Fujita Phenomena for Fractional Hardy-Hénon Type Parabolic Equations
summary:
We study the lifespan of mild solutions to the fractional semilinear parabolic Cauchy problem with a Hardy--Hénon-type weight \[
  u_t + (-Δ)^s u = |x|^{-γ}\,|u|^p,
  \qquad (t,x)\in(0,\infty)\times\mathbb{R}^N,
  \qquad u(0,x)=\varepsilon\,u_0(x), \] where $0<s<1$, $0\leγ<\min(2s,N)$, $p>1$ and $u_0\in L^1\cap L^\infty$ with $\int_{\mathbb{R}^N}u_0(x)\,dx>0$. Setting \[
  p_F \;:=\; 1+\frac{2s-γ}{N}, \] we prove that the lifespan $T_\varepsilon$ obeys, for every sufficiently small $\varepsilon>0$, \[
  T_\varepsilon \;\approx\;
  \begin{cases}
  \varepsilon^{-\,β^{-1}},& 1<p<p_F,\\[1mm]
  \exp\!\big(C\,\varepsilon^{-(p-1)}\big),& p=p_F,\\[1mm]
  +\infty,& p>p_F,
  \end{cases}
  \qquad
  β\;=\;\frac{(2s-γ)-N(p-1)}{2s(p-1)}. \] The lower bound rests on fractional heat-kernel estimates and an $L^1$--$L^\infty$ Hardy-type interpolation inequality; the upper bound is obtained by testing the equation against the backward fractional heat kernel, a globally defined positive weight for which $(-Δ)^s$ is controlled everywhere and the linear terms cancel identically by self-adjointness. This circumvents the compactly supported cutoffs of the classical test-function method, which are incompatible with a nonlocal operator. The exponent $β$ is sharp; for $γ=0$ it reduces to the fractional Lee--Ni exponent $\frac{1}{p-1}-\frac{N}{2s}$. To the best of our knowledge, these results are new even for $γ=0.$ We also establish a large-data lifespan law, sharp lower bounds on the blow-up rate together with a conditional Type-I upper bound, a conditional self-similar profile result.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the lifespan of mild solutions to a fractional semilinear parabolic equation with a Hardy-Hénon weight, proving sharp lifespan estimates (blow-up time) depending on the exponent p relative to a critical exponent p_F = 1 + (2s-γ)/N.
AI.motivation: To understand how the interplay between fractional diffusion (nonlocal operator (-Δ)^s) and a singular Hardy-Hénon-type weight |x|^{-γ} affects the blow-up behavior and lifespan of solutions, extending classical results to the nonlocal setting.
AI.method: Lower bound uses fractional heat-kernel estimates and an L^1-L^∞ Hardy-type interpolation inequality; upper bound tests the equation against the backward fractional heat kernel (a globally defined positive weight), circumventing the incompatibility of compactly supported cutoffs with nonlocal operators.
AI.result: For small ε>0, lifespan T_ε ≈ ε^{-β^{-1}} for 1<p<p_F, ≈ exp(C ε^{-(p-1)}) for p=p_F, and ∞ for p>p_F, where β = ((2s-γ)-N(p-1))/(2s(p-1)). The exponent β is sharp and reduces to the fractional Lee-Ni exponent for γ=0.
AI.conclusion: Sharp lifespan estimates are established, along with large-data lifespan law, sharp blow-up rate lower bounds, a conditional Type-I upper bound, and a conditional self-similar profile result. Results are new even for γ=0.

title: Numerical analysis of first-order mean field games under displacement monotonicity
summary: We introduce a particle method for the numerical approximation of time-dependent first-order Mean Field Games (MFGs) systems with non-separable, displacement monotone Hamiltonians and terminal costs, for arbitrary time-horizons and (possibly) singular initial player distributions in $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d)$. The numerical scheme is based on an implicit Euler discretization in time and sampling in space of the characteristic Hamiltonian/Pontryagin system associated with the continuous MFGs system. We prove convergence of the approximations of the player distribution in the $L^{\infty}(\mathcal{W}_2)$-metric and the approximations for the gradient of the value function along optimal trajectories in the $L^{\infty}{(L^2)}$-norm as the number of spatial samples tends to infinity jointly with the temporal time-step vanishing. The error bound that we establish for this convergence further implies rates of convergence of the scheme for a range of spatial sampling techniques. Provided that the Lagrangian and terminal costs are additionally locally Lipschitz continuous, we also establish an asymptotic error bound in the $L^{\infty}(L^1)$-norm for the approximations of the value function along optimal trajectories. This is the first work in the literature on rigorous numerical approximation and analysis of first-order MFG systems that handles non-separable Hamiltonians and potentially singular initial agent distributions for arbitrary long time horizons. We illustrate the performance of the scheme in numerical experiments for a range of initial agent distributions, time horizons and space dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a particle method for numerically approximating time-dependent first-order Mean Field Games (MFGs) with non-separable, displacement monotone Hamiltonians and terminal costs, handling arbitrary time horizons and singular initial distributions. The scheme uses implicit Euler time discretization and spatial sampling of the Hamiltonian/Pontryagin system. Convergence is proven in L∞(W₂)-metric for player distribution and L∞(L²)-norm for gradient of value function, with error bounds and convergence rates. Additional L∞(L¹)-norm error bounds are established for value functions under local Lipschitz conditions. This is the first work providing rigorous numerical analysis for first-order MFGs with non-separable Hamiltonians and potentially singular initial distributions over arbitrary long time horizons.
AI.motivation: To develop and rigorously analyze a numerical approximation scheme for first-order Mean Field Games systems that can handle non-separable Hamiltonians, displacement monotone costs, arbitrary time horizons, and potentially singular initial player distributions—a gap in existing numerical MFG literature.
AI.method: Particle method based on implicit Euler discretization in time and spatial sampling of the characteristic Hamiltonian/Pontryagin system associated with the continuous MFGs system.
AI.result: Proved convergence of player distribution approximations in L∞(W₂)-metric and gradient of value function approximations in L∞(L²)-norm as spatial samples →∞ and time step →0. Established convergence rates for various spatial sampling techniques, and asymptotic L∞(L¹)-norm error bounds for value function approximations under local Lipschitz continuity. Numerical experiments demonstrate performance across different initial distributions, time horizons, and space dimensions.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work providing rigorous numerical approximation and analysis for first-order MFG systems that handles non-separable Hamiltonians and potentially singular initial agent distributions for arbitrary long time horizons, with proven convergence guarantees.

title: Neural Networks for Inverse Design of Cascaded-Mode Near-Field Landscapes
summary: Structuring optical near-fields is important for applications in microscopy and nanoparticle manipulation. Traditionally, near-fields are structured using antenna nanostructures that locally convert propagating far-fields into bound near-fields. Recently, a remote structuring approach was proposed using cascaded mode interference in a multimode waveguide. However, determining the complex coefficients of the optimal modal combination needed to obtain specific near-fields remains a challenge. We address this inverse design problem using artificial neural networks. We model the relationship between the design parameters and near-field landscapes using multilayer neural networks. After training, these networks are used for gradient-based optimization to reconstruct target near-field profiles. We implement this methodology to design longitudinal and lateral field variations. Our approach designs simple and complex longitudinal landscapes, demonstrating accurate prediction and flexibility. Lateral field reconstruction is more challenging but improved with training data selection and augmentation. This work establishes deep learning as an efficient and scalable framework for cascaded-mode near-field inverse design.

AI.tldr: This paper uses artificial neural networks to solve the inverse design problem of structuring optical near-fields through cascaded mode interference in multimode waveguides, enabling accurate reconstruction of both simple and complex near-field landscapes.
AI.motivation: Determining the optimal modal combination coefficients for obtaining specific near-fields in cascaded-mode interference is challenging; existing methods lack an efficient solution for this inverse design problem.
AI.method: The authors model the relationship between design parameters and near-field landscapes using multilayer neural networks, then apply gradient-based optimization on trained networks to reconstruct target near-field profiles.
AI.result: The approach accurately predicts and designs simple and complex longitudinal landscapes; lateral field reconstruction is more challenging but improved with data selection and augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning provides an efficient and scalable framework for cascaded-mode near-field inverse design.

title: Latent thermal instability
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. To be submitted, comments are welcome
summary: Multiscale temperature fluctuations are abundant in the intracluster medium (ICM) outside of galaxy cluster cores ($\sim 100~{\rm kpc}$). Their origin is often attributed to turbulent stirring by subhalos or accreting baryons crossing the virial radius. However, their apparent resistance to mixing and thermal conduction in a collisional medium has not been explained. We propose a new mechanism by which steady-state temperature fluctuations can form and persist outside the cluster core. Local thermal instability, or Field instability, is used to explain filamentary condensates in cluster cores but is usually dismissed outside them because thermal conduction should suppress instability. In weakly collisional or collisionless plasmas, however, thermal conduction can be anomalously suppressed by heat-flux-driven plasma instabilities triggered in presence of a local magnetic field, leading to two effects: (i) condensates form in a new parameter regime that overlaps with conditions outside the core, and (ii) condensates reach a steady state as in the hydrodynamic limit.
