This extends the regime of instability-driven fluctuations to over $\gtrsim50\%$ (depending on hot plasma temperature) of the cluster. We use one-dimensional hydrodynamic simulations of condensates to test our analytical ideas.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that heat-flux-driven plasma instabilities can suppress thermal conduction in the intracluster medium (ICM) outside cluster cores, allowing temperature fluctuations to persist via local thermal instability, extending the regime of such fluctuations to over 50% of the cluster volume.
AI.motivation: To explain the origin and persistence of multiscale temperature fluctuations observed in the ICM outside galaxy cluster cores, which cannot be explained by standard turbulent stirring or thermal conduction suppression mechanisms.
AI.method: Analytical modeling of heat-flux-driven plasma instabilities that suppress thermal conduction in weakly collisional/collisionless plasmas, combined with 1D hydrodynamic simulations of condensates to test the proposed mechanism.
AI.result: The new mechanism extends the regime of instability-driven temperature fluctuations to over ~50% of the cluster volume (depending on hot plasma temperature), and condensates reach a steady state similar to the hydrodynamic limit.
AI.conclusion: Heat-flux-driven plasma instabilities enable local thermal instability to sustain temperature fluctuations outside cluster cores, explaining their observed persistence despite expectations of rapid mixing and conduction.

title: Determining potentials from the scattering map of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation
summary: For a time dependent Schrödinger equation, the scattering map is the map sending the asymptotic profile of a solution as $t \to-\infty$ to its asymptotic profile as $t\to+\infty$. In this paper we show that, for a certain class of metrics, the scattering maps and Poisson operators associated to two Schrödinger operators on the same curved space only differ by a compact operator on a critical level if and only if the two potentials are equal.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that for a class of metrics on curved spaces, two Schrödinger operators have scattering maps and Poisson operators differing by a compact operator (at a critical energy level) if and only if their potentials are identical.
AI.motivation: To establish a uniqueness result: scattering data (scattering maps and Poisson operators) uniquely determine the potential for Schrödinger operators on curved spaces, specifically at critical energy levels.
AI.method: The authors analyze the time-dependent Schrödinger equation on curved spaces with a certain class of metrics. They compare scattering maps and Poisson operators associated with two different potentials and prove that a compact difference at a critical level forces the potentials to be equal.
AI.result: The scattering maps and Poisson operators of two Schrödinger operators differ by a compact operator on a critical level if and only if the two potentials are equal.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a rigidity result: the scattering map (or Poisson operator) at a critical energy level uniquely determines the potential for Schrödinger operators on curved spaces under the considered metric class.

title: Accelerated sampling using SamAdams variable timesteps and position-adaptive Langevin dynamics
summary: We introduce an accelerated Langevin-based sampling method that is based on two complementary devices: \emph{SamAdams} adaptive timestepping, which automatically shrinks the effective integration step in stiff regions of phase space using a relaxed stiffness monitor, and \emph{position-adaptive Langevin} (PAL) dynamics, which concentrates friction along the local force direction while preserving the canonical distribution as the exact invariant measure. The resulting combined scheme (SA-PAL) is implemented in a palindromic integrator which requires only one force evaluation per iteration through suitable organisation of the integration steps and by exploiting the rank-one-plus-scalar structure of the PAL friction tensor. We test the method on various model problems: the Rosenbrock function, a thin entropic channel, the Mueller-Brown potential, and a Bayesian parameterisation problem with a sparsity-inducing shrinkage prior. On the Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials mixing rates are improved by 1.5-3 times compared to fixed stepsize integration. Efficiency gains of more than an order of magnitude are documented in the other examples.

AI.tldr: SA-PAL is a new accelerated Langevin sampling method combining adaptive timestepping (SamAdams) with position-adaptive Langevin dynamics, achieving 1.5-3x improved mixing on standard potentials and >10x efficiency gains on entropic and Bayesian problems.
AI.motivation: Standard Langevin sampling methods suffer from slow mixing in stiff regions of phase space and inefficient exploration when friction is isotropic; the paper aims to address both issues simultaneously.
AI.method: Combines SamAdams adaptive timestepping (relaxed stiffness monitor to shrink steps in stiff regions) with Position-Adaptive Langevin (PAL) dynamics (friction concentrates along local force direction, preserving canonical distribution). Implemented in a palindromic integrator requiring only one force evaluation per iteration.
AI.result: 1.5-3x improvements in mixing rates on Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials; >10x efficiency gains on entropic channel and Bayesian parameterization with shrinkage prior.
AI.conclusion: SA-PAL significantly outperforms fixed-stepsize Langevin methods, especially in problems with stiff dynamics or anisotropic energy landscapes, while maintaining computational efficiency via the rank-one-plus-scalar friction structure.

title: Self-improving properties for the fractional $p$-Laplacian via nonlinear commutators
summary: We investigate a class of nonlocal equations whose leading operator is modeled on either the fractional $p$-Laplacian or the regional fractional $p$-Laplacian, $p \in (1,\infty)$. We prove local self-improving properties of weak solutions to the fractional $p$-Laplacian in the case $p\in(1,\infty)$ with non-integrable right-hand side, as well as to the regional fractional $p$-Laplacian in the subquadratic case $1 < p < 2$, by extending the nonlinear commutator estimates developed by Schikorra (Math. Ann. 366 (1-2):695--720, 2016).

AI.tldr: This paper proves local self-improving properties (higher integrability) for weak solutions of nonlocal equations involving fractional p-Laplacian and regional fractional p-Laplacian operators, using extended nonlinear commutator estimates.
AI.motivation: To establish self-improving (higher integrability) properties for weak solutions of nonlocal equations with non-integrable right-hand sides, extending existing techniques to the full range p∈(1,∞) for fractional p-Laplacian and subquadratic case 1<p<2 for regional fractional p-Laplacian.
AI.method: Extends nonlinear commutator estimates originally developed by Schikorra (2016) to handle the fractional p-Laplacian and regional fractional p-Laplacian operators.
AI.result: Local self-improving properties (higher integrability) are proven for weak solutions: (i) fractional p-Laplacian for all p∈(1,∞) with non-integrable right-hand side, (ii) regional fractional p-Laplacian in the subquadratic case 1<p<2.
AI.conclusion: The nonlinear commutator approach can be successfully extended to prove regularity beyond natural integrability for a broad class of nonlocal nonlinear problems.

title: Incremental Tensor-Train Compression from Streaming TT-Formatted Data: Applications to Reduced-Order Modeling
summary: High-dimensional tensor data streams arise naturally in scientific and engineering applications, such as simulations of kinetic equations and quantum systems, where samples become available sequentially and are often already represented in compressed low-rank tensor formats. Existing streaming tensor-train (TT) algorithms typically construct or update representations from dense tensor data or randomized sketches. However, when high-dimensional data are generated directly in TT or related low-rank formats, reconstructing dense tensors solely for the purpose of compression is unnecessary and computationally prohibitive. We develop a deterministic incremental TT compression algorithm that operates directly on streaming TT-formatted data. Given a new TT tensor, the proposed method updates an accumulated TT representation through core-wise projection, residual orthogonalization, and adaptive enrichment, retaining only the complementary information that cannot be represented within a prescribed tolerance. By operating entirely at the level of TT cores, the algorithm avoids reconstructing either the incoming tensor or the accumulated full tensor. We establish approximation error bounds for the proposed incremental approach. Moreover, we show that the accumulated TT representation corresponds to a compressed analogue of standard proper orthogonal decomposition for full-order snapshot data, enabling reduced-order models to be constructed directly from streaming low-rank solution data through operations on TT cores, without first reconstructing full snapshots. Numerical experiments on parametric radiative transfer equations demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable reconstruction accuracy with substantially reduced wall time and yields efficient and accurate ROMs directly from compressed low-rank data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a deterministic incremental TT compression algorithm that operates directly on streaming TT-formatted data, avoiding full tensor reconstruction, with proven error bounds and demonstrated efficiency on radiative transfer equations.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional tensor data streams arise in scientific applications (e.g., kinetic equations, quantum systems) where samples arrive sequentially in low-rank TT format. Existing streaming TT algorithms require dense tensor reconstruction or randomized sketches, which is computationally prohibitive when data is already in compressed format.
AI.method: A deterministic incremental TT compression algorithm that updates an accumulated TT representation through core-wise projection, residual orthogonalization, and adaptive enrichment, operating entirely at the TT core level without reconstructing full tensors.
AI.result: Established approximation error bounds; numerical experiments on parametric radiative transfer equations show comparable reconstruction accuracy with substantially reduced wall time and efficient ROMs directly from compressed low-rank data.
AI.conclusion: The accumulated TT representation serves as a compressed analogue of standard POD for full-order snapshots, enabling ROM construction directly from streaming low-rank solution data via TT core operations, avoiding full snapshot reconstruction.

title: Almost Lipschitz regularity for solutions of elliptic equations with discontinuous coefficients
summary:
We are interested in the local higher integrability of solutions to elliptic equations with linear growth of the form
  $$-\text{ div}A(x,Du)=f(x). $$ Under a Besov regularity assumption both on the partial map $x \mapsto A(x,ξ)$ and the datum $f$, we prove that the solutions are almost Lipschitz continuous, i.e. their gradients belong locally to $L^q$, for any finite exponent $q$. In turn, solutions are locally $γ$-Hölder continuous, for every $γ\in (0,1)$. The difficulty arising from the lack of an explicit second variation for the problem is overcome by testing the equation with a function proportional to a power of the finite difference quotient of the solution. To the best of our knowledge, this technique is used in this context for the first time. We also provide an example showing the sharpness of our result in the scale of Lebesgue spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that solutions to elliptic equations with linear growth become almost Lipschitz (gradients in L^q for any finite q) under Besov regularity assumptions on the coefficient A and datum f, using a novel finite difference quotient technique.
AI.motivation: To improve the regularity theory for elliptic equations by showing higher integrability of solutions under weaker (Besov) regularity assumptions on coefficients and data, bridging the gap toward Lipschitz continuity.
AI.method: Testing the equation with a function proportional to a power of the finite difference quotient of the solution, a technique used for the first time in this context to overcome the lack of explicit second variation.
AI.result: Solutions' gradients belong locally to L^q for any finite exponent q (almost Lipschitz), and solutions are locally γ-Hölder continuous for every γ∈(0,1). The result is sharp in the Lebesgue scale.
AI.conclusion: Besov regularity on A(x,ξ) and f yields almost Lipschitz regularity; the technique is novel and the result optimal.

title: Solving Shifted Systems for Quasiseparable Matrices
summary:
In this paper we develop fast numerical algorithms for solving shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices.
  A combination of Givens and hyperbolic plane rotations is used to update the Cholesky-type factorization of the input quasiseparable matrix by
  determining a factorization of its shifted version of the form $LDL^T$, where $L$ is lower triangular and $D$ is a signature matrix.
  If the shifted matrix is also definite then the Cholesky factorization of the shifted matrix is computed in a stable way by using orthogonal transformations. Since quasiseparability is maintained under diagonal shifting, a fast variant of the updating procedure using
  computations with generators is also devised. Numerical experiments show the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper develops fast algorithms for solving shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices, using Givens and hyperbolic rotations to update Cholesky-type factorizations efficiently.
AI.motivation: To design fast and stable numerical algorithms for solving shifted linear systems involving semidefinite quasiseparable matrices, which arise in various applications like signal processing and control theory.
AI.method: The method combines Givens and hyperbolic plane rotations to update the Cholesky-type factorization (LDL^T) of quasiseparable matrices under diagonal shifting. A fast variant using generator computations is also developed, leveraging the property that quasiseparability is preserved under diagonal shifts.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a stable, fast, and structure-exploiting method for solving shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices.

title: Exact Blowup Analysis for the Weak-Advection Hou--Li Model
summary: We study self-similar singularity formation for the one-dimensional weak-advection Hou--Li model, a reduced model motivated by the axisymmetric Euler equations. In the periodic setting, we construct exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for $2/3<a<1$, with profiles that are neither focusing nor expanding. In the whole-space setting with a Neumann condition, we construct exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for the full range $0<a\leq1$, with profiles of focusing, non-expanding/non-focusing, or expanding form depending on the sign of the self-similar scaling parameter. The construction is based on a fixed-point formulation near the origin, followed by an ODE extension argument. We also establish regularity, asymptotic behavior, monotonicity properties of the profiles, and uniqueness up to the natural scaling invariance.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for the 1D weak-advection Hou-Li model, a reduced model for axisymmetric Euler equations, across different parameter regimes and boundary conditions, establishing regularity, asymptotics, monotonicity, and uniqueness of the profiles.
AI.motivation: To understand singularity formation in fluid dynamics, specifically motivated by the axisymmetric Euler equations, by studying self-similar blowup in the Hou-Li reduced model.
AI.method: Based on a fixed-point formulation near the origin followed by an ODE extension argument, constructing exact self-similar profiles for different scaling regimes.
AI.result: Exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions constructed for 2/3 < a < 1 (periodic) and 0 < a ≤ 1 (whole-space with Neumann condition), with profiles exhibiting focusing, non-expanding/non-focusing, or expanding behavior depending on parameters.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous construction of blowup profiles with proven regularity, asymptotic behavior, monotonicity, and uniqueness up to scaling invariance.

title: An Arbitrary-Lagrangian-Eulerian solver for relativistic detonation waves
summary: In this paper we study the dynamics of relativistic detonation waves theoretically and numerically. The reaction is physically accounted for by an extra term in the definition of the total energy density and by an additional equation for the evolution of the mass fraction of the reactant, while leaving formally unmodified the equations of mass and energy-momentum conservation. In this way, the Rankine-Hugoniot relations maintain the same formal structure of the inert version. For the numerical solution we use a second order finite volume ALE scheme with TVD reconstruction, where the mesh velocity is chosen equal to the shock speed. We also adopt a locally implicit algorithm for the treatment of potentially stiff reaction source terms that arise in the equation of the reactant. We furthermore propose a particularly efficient algorithm for the conversion from the conserved to the primitive variables, which for the relativistic Euler equations is known to be nontrivial. Following this approach, we can successfully solve the Zel'dovich-von Neumann-Doering profile of a relativistsic detonation wave, up to Lorentz factors of the shock front $γ_S\sim 7$. Our analysis allowed us to highlight a new special relativistic effect, which has remained unnoticed so far. While in Newtonian detonations the Zel'dovich pressure jump decreases monotonically with the mass flux through the shock front, in the relativistic case it shows a minimum and then rises monotonically as a function of the mass flux. This may have interesting physical implications on the amount of energy that can be extracted from a relativistic detonation wave.

AI.tldr: A theoretical and numerical study of relativistic detonation waves, revealing a new special relativistic effect where the Zel'dovich pressure jump shows a minimum and then rises with mass flux (unlike the monotonic decrease in Newtonian detonations).
AI.motivation: To understand and accurately simulate relativistic detonation waves, particularly their dynamics and any novel relativistic effects that differ from Newtonian detonations.
AI.method: A second-order finite volume ALE scheme with TVD reconstruction, mesh velocity set to shock speed, locally implicit algorithm for stiff reaction source terms, and an efficient algorithm for converting conserved to primitive variables in the relativistic Euler equations.
AI.result: Successfully solved the Zel'dovich-von Neumann-Doering profile of relativistic detonation waves up to Lorentz factors of γ_S~7. Discovered a new effect: the Zel'dovich pressure jump first decreases then rises with mass flux in the relativistic case, unlike the monotonic decrease in Newtonian detonations.
AI.conclusion: The new relativistic effect may have interesting physical implications for the amount of energy extractable from relativistic detonation waves.

title: Global well-posedness for general parabolic Anderson model on the whole plane
summary:
For every $ 0<κ<\sqrt 5-2$, we prove global existence for the two-dimensional generalized parabolic Anderson model on the whole space $\mathbb R^2$ with nonlinearity $F\in C_b^2(\mathbb R)$, driven by an enhanced noise $(η,Ψ)$. Here the noise $η$ has polynomially weighted spatial Besov--Hölder regularity $-1-κ$, and $Ψ$ is the corresponding renormalized second-order object. If $F''$ is globally Lipschitz, the solution is unique.
  The proof combines a weight-compatible annular high-low decomposition with a paracontrolled transport representation. The final remainder is estimated simultaneously in weighted $L^\infty$ norm and in a higher order weighted parabolic Hölder norm, with strictly different polynomial weights. This weight gap absorbs the polynomial losses arising from the enhanced noise and the transport coefficient. Several key refinements of the earlier works allow the maximum-principle and Schauder estimates to yield a global a priori bound for a larger range of $κ$. Uniqueness is proved in a time-dependent exponentially weighted topology.

AI.tldr: The paper proves global existence (and uniqueness under stronger conditions) for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model with rough noise, using a weighted decomposition and paracontrolled transport method to handle polynomial losses.
AI.motivation: To establish global well-posedness for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model with rough noise (regularity below -1) on the whole space ℝ², extending existing results to a larger range of the parameter κ.
AI.method: Combines a weight-compatible annular high-low decomposition with a paracontrolled transport representation; uses weighted L∞ and parabolic Hölder norms with different polynomial weights; applies maximum-principle and Schauder estimates with key refinements.
AI.result: Proves global existence for all 0 < κ < √5 - 2, and uniqueness when F'' is globally Lipschitz, for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model driven by enhanced noise with regularity -1-κ.
AI.conclusion: The weight gap technique successfully absorbs polynomial losses from enhanced noise and transport coefficients, enabling global a priori bounds for a larger κ range than previous works.

title: Fast summation on rectangular cuboids with arbitrary periodicity in the DMK framework
summary: Dual-space multilevel kernel-splitting (DMK) is a fast summation framework that combines ideas from the fast multipole method, Ewald summation, and multilevel summation. Originally formulated for free-space problems, and later extended to fully periodic problems on a cube, it decomposes the kernel interaction into a smooth global contribution and a hierarchy of localized interactions evaluated on an octree. We extend DMK to problems on rectangular cuboids with periodic boundary conditions in one, two, or three coordinate directions. The periodization leverages the fact that interactions on all tree levels below the root are localized, allowing for their evaluation with minimal modification on a cubical tiling of the domain. The remaining smooth root-level far-field contribution is evaluated in Fourier space, with Fourier series in the periodic directions and Fourier integrals in the free directions. For reduced periodicity, truncated kernels are used to regularize singular and near-singular Fourier kernels, yielding rapidly convergent trapezoidal discretizations and a unified treatment of all periodicities. For large-aspect-ratio cuboids, the root-level sum can be accelerated using the fast Fourier transform. We validate the method for the electrostatic potential and Stokeslet, stresslet and rotlet potentials, for all periodicities and a wide range of aspect ratios. Numerical experiments show that the periodization adds only a small overhead to the original free-space DMK algorithm, also for high-aspect-ratio cuboids. The resulting method provides a framework for applying DMK to problems with mixed periodicity on rectangular cuboids, and extends naturally to other non-oscillatory kernels for which a kernel split is available.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Dual-space Multilevel Kernel-splitting (DMK) method from free-space and cubic periodic problems to rectangular cuboids with periodic boundary conditions in one, two, or three directions, using Fourier methods for the root-level interaction and truncated kernels for regularization, with validation across various potentials and aspect ratios.
AI.motivation: To extend the existing DMK fast summation framework, which was limited to free-space and fully periodic cubic domains, to handle rectangular cuboids with mixed periodicities (1D, 2D, or 3D periodic), enabling broader applicability to realistic problems with non-cubic geometries and reduced periodicity.
AI.method: The method periodizes DMK by evaluating localized tree-level interactions on a cubical tiling with minimal changes, while the smooth root-level far-field contribution is computed in Fourier space—using Fourier series for periodic directions and Fourier integrals for free directions. Truncated kernels regularize singular/near-singular Fourier kernels for reduced periodicity, and FFT acceleration is applied for large-aspect-ratio cuboids.
AI.result: Numerical validation for electrostatic potential, Stokeslet, stresslet, and rotlet potentials across all periodicities and a wide range of aspect ratios shows that the periodization introduces only small computational overhead compared to the original free-space DMK, even for high-aspect-ratio cuboids.
AI.conclusion: The extended DMK provides an efficient and unified framework for problems with mixed periodicity on rectangular cuboids, and naturally generalizes to other non-oscillatory kernels with an available kernel split.

title: Mixed Third-Order Flux Laws for Dual Cascade in the Stochastic SQG Equation
summary: We study dual-cascade flux laws for the stochastic forced--dissipative surface quasi-geostrophic (SQG) equation on a large periodic box. For statistically stationary solutions, under a weak anomalous dissipation assumption, we derive rigorous mixed third-order structure-function laws for the dual cascade: a Yaglom-type law for the direct cascade of surface potential energy (SPE) and an antisymmetrized mixed flux law for the inverse cascade of the Hamiltonian. In particular, the inverse Hamiltonian law appears to be new even as an explicit third-order structure-function relation. We also prove Onsager-type obstruction results showing that sufficiently regular stationary families cannot sustain the corresponding non-zero fluxes: $B^s_{3,\infty}$-regularity above the Onsager threshold $1/3$ rules out the direct SPE flux, while sufficient low-frequency Besov regularity rules out the inverse Hamiltonian flux. These results provide a rigorous formulation of the SQG dual-cascade phenomenology in a stochastic stationary setting.

AI.tldr: This paper derives rigorous third-order structure-function flux laws for dual cascades in stochastically forced SQG equations, including a new inverse Hamiltonian law, and proves Onsager-type regularity thresholds that prevent nonzero fluxes.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical formulation of the dual-cascade phenomenology (direct SPE cascade and inverse Hamiltonian cascade) for the stochastic forced-dissipative SQG equation, including deriving explicit flux laws and identifying regularity thresholds.
AI.method: The authors work with statistically stationary solutions of the stochastically forced SQG equation on a large periodic box, assuming weak anomalous dissipation. They derive mixed third-order structure-function laws: a Yaglom-type law for the direct SPE cascade and an antisymmetrized mixed flux law for the inverse Hamiltonian cascade. Onsager-type obstruction results are proved using Besov regularity spaces.
AI.result: Rigorous third-order structure-function flux laws for both direct SPE cascade and inverse Hamiltonian cascade are established (the inverse Hamiltonian law is new). Furthermore, it is shown that $B^s_{3,\infty}$-regularity above the Onsager threshold $1/3$ precludes the direct SPE flux, and sufficient low-frequency Besov regularity precludes the inverse Hamiltonian flux.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for SQG dual-cascade phenomenology in a stochastic stationary setting, establishing both flux laws and sharp regularity thresholds analogous to Onsager's theorem for 3D Euler turbulence.

title: On Fourier Phase Retrieval from Differential Intensity Measurements with Applications to Wavefront Sensing
summary: In this paper, we consider Fourier phase retrieval from differential intensity measurements, i.e., the problem of determining the phase of a complex-valued function from a series of intensity measurements differing only by slight modulations in Fourier domain. These modulations may be induced by optical elements such as prisms or phase plates, or via spatial-light modulators. Generalizing the principles behind the transport of intensity equation, we show that given such differential intensity measurements, the phase can be determined as the solution of certain partial differential or integro-differential equations. This is then used to design efficient reconstruction algorithms for a number of Fourier-type wavefront sensors, such as the pyramid wavefront sensor, commonly used in adaptive optics. Numerical experiments illustrate the usefulness of our proposed approach.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method for Fourier phase retrieval from differential intensity measurements, showing the phase can be determined via partial differential or integro-differential equations, with applications to wavefront sensors like the pyramid wavefront sensor in adaptive optics.
AI.motivation: To solve Fourier phase retrieval from differential intensity measurements (determining phase from intensity measurements with slight Fourier modulations), generalizing transport of intensity equation principles.
AI.method: Generalizes transport of intensity equation principles to show phase can be determined as the solution of partial differential or integro-differential equations from differential intensity measurements.
AI.result: Efficient reconstruction algorithms are designed for Fourier-type wavefront sensors (e.g., pyramid wavefront sensor), with numerical experiments demonstrating usefulness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is effective for phase retrieval in adaptive optics applications using differential intensity measurements.

title: Quantitative uniform resolvent estimates
summary: We derive quantitative uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators on the half-line with inverse-square potentials, which provide a sharp behaviour in the limit of large coupling. Our approach is based on a matrix representation of the boundary value of a weighted resolvent. The partial wave decomposition then turns these one-dimensional channel estimates into explicit weighted resolvent estimates for the Laplacian, its inverse-square potential perturbations and for the magnetic Laplacian with an Aharonov--Bohm potential. We also obtain exact Simon-type identities for the imaginary parts of the weighted resolvents of these operators.

AI.tldr: Derives quantitative uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators with inverse-square potentials on the half-line, obtaining sharp large-coupling behavior via a matrix representation approach, and extends results to Laplacians and magnetic Laplacians with Aharonov-Bohm potentials.
AI.motivation: To obtain sharp quantitative resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators with inverse-square potentials in the large-coupling limit, which have not been previously established.
AI.method: Uses a matrix representation of the boundary value of a weighted resolvent, combined with partial wave decomposition to turn one-dimensional channel estimates into explicit weighted resolvent estimates.
AI.result: Sharp quantitative uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators with inverse-square potentials on the half-line, extended to Laplacian perturbations and magnetic Laplacian with Aharonov-Bohm potential. Also obtains exact Simon-type identities for imaginary parts of weighted resolvents.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides explicit, sharp estimates in the large-coupling regime and yields Simon-type identities for these operators.

title: De la Vallée Poussin type approximation for solving some Fredholm integral equations
summary: In the present paper, we introduce a numerical method for second-kind Fredholm integral equations (FIEs) based on de la Vallée Poussin-type (VP) polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros. This class of approximations offers several advantages over classical Lagrange interpolation at the same nodes. In particular, it guarantees uniformly bounded Lebesgue constants in suitable weighted function spaces and provides near-best uniform approximation for functions in these spaces, while also significantly mitigating the Gibbs phenomenon. We show how these properties can be exploited in the numerical solution of FIEs. In particular, the proposed approach effectively handles functions with possible algebraic endpoint singularities and kernel functions featuring weak singularities or highly oscillatory behavior. Under suitable assumptions, we prove stability and convergence of the method in weighted uniform spaces. Furthermore, we develop an efficient implementation based on the solution of a well-conditioned linear system. Numerical results confirm the theoretical error estimates and show that the proposed method achieves higher local accuracy than the corresponding Lagrange-based projection method.

AI.tldr: A numerical method for second-kind Fredholm integral equations using de la Vallée Poussin-type polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros, offering better stability, reduced Gibbs phenomenon, and handling of singular/oscillatory kernels.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of classical Lagrange interpolation (unbounded Lebesgue constants, Gibbs phenomenon) when solving Fredholm integral equations, especially for functions with endpoint singularities or weakly singular/oscillatory kernels.
AI.method: Uses de la Vallée Poussin-type polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros, which provide uniformly bounded Lebesgue constants and near-best uniform approximation. The method is implemented via a well-conditioned linear system and analyzed in weighted uniform spaces.
AI.result: Stability and convergence proved in weighted uniform spaces. Numerical results confirm theoretical error bounds and show higher local accuracy than Lagrange-based projection methods.
AI.conclusion: The VP-type polynomial approach is an effective, stable, and accurate numerical method for second-kind FIEs, particularly beneficial for problems with singularities or oscillatory behavior.

title: Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Solving Integro and Fractional PDEs
summary: Quantum neural networks have emerged as powerful models for approximating nonlinear functions. Yet their use in solving integro-differential equations (IDEs) and fractional integro-partial differential equations (FIPDEs), which involve inherently nonlocal operators, remains unexplored. This work introduces a quantum physics-informed neural network (QPINN) framework that combines a quantum neural network with the governing equations of general nonlinear IDEs and FIPDEs. The proposed quantum network uses an affine feature map and variational quantum circuits to produce trial solutions with explicit trigonometric structure. We prove a quantitative $L^{2}(μ)$ universal approximation theorem for this architecture, achieving a convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(n^{-1/2})$. This extends classical Fourier approximation theory to quantum circuits for physics-informed learning. We propose two QPINN variants: the numerical-quadrature QPINN (N-QPINN), which handles nonlocal integrals and fractional operators via high-order numerical quadrature while computing local derivatives through automatic differentiation of quantum trial solutions; and the auxiliary-function QPINN (A-QPINN), which eliminates numerical quadrature by introducing auxiliary variables that reformulate each integro-differential equation as an equivalent coupled system of partial differential equations, enabling a multi-output quantum neural network to simultaneously represent the solution and its associated variables. A series of numerical experiments demonstrates that the proposed QPINN framework accurately captures the behavior of nonlinear IDEs and FIPDEs and outperforms classical physics-informed neural networks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a quantum physics-informed neural network (QPINN) framework for solving nonlinear integro-differential equations (IDEs) and fractional integro-partial differential equations (FIPDEs). It proposes two variants: N-QPINN (using numerical quadrature) and A-QPINN (using auxiliary variables). The quantum neural network uses affine feature maps and variational circuits, and a universal approximation theorem with convergence rate O(n^{-1/2}) is proved. Numerical experiments show QPINN outperforms classical PINNs.
AI.motivation: Quantum neural networks have been used for approximating nonlinear functions, but their application to solving integro-differential equations (IDEs) and fractional integro-partial differential equations (FIPDEs), which involve nonlocal operators, had not been explored. This work aims to fill this gap.
AI.method: The QPINN framework combines a quantum neural network (with affine feature maps and variational quantum circuits) with governing equations of IDEs/FIPDEs. Two variants are proposed: N-QPINN (uses numerical quadrature for integrals/fractional operators, automatic differentiation for local derivatives) and A-QPINN (introduces auxiliary variables to reformulate each IDE as a coupled PDE system, using multi-output quantum network). A universal approximation theorem with O(n^{-1/2}) convergence rate is proved.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that QPINN accurately captures behavior of nonlinear IDEs and FIPDEs and outperforms classical physics-informed neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The QPINN framework is effective for solving IDEs and FIPDEs, extending quantum physics-informed learning to nonlocal operators, and outperforms classical PINNs.

title: Hierarchical Muon: Tiled Newton-Schulz Updates for Efficient Muon Optimization
summary: Muon-type optimizers construct update directions for dense neural-network weights by applying a finite Newton-Schulz map to momentum-gradient matrices. For an $H \times W$ matrix, with $r=\min\{H,W\}$ and $s=\max\{H,W\}$, $K$ steps of the full-matrix Newton-Schulz update require $O(r^2 s K)$ work and couple all rows and columns through repeated Gram matrix products. We introduce Hierarchical Muon (HiMuon), a tiled Newton-Schulz scheme for Muon-type optimization. HiMuon partitions each momentum-gradient matrix into $T \times T$ tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map independently to each tile, and reassembles the results. For finite $T$ below the matrix dimensions, HiMuon defines a local matrix-function map rather than a convergent approximation to the full-matrix update: spectral interactions are preserved within tiles and discarded across tile boundaries. For fixed finite $T$, the leading Newton-Schulz work decreases to $O(H W T K)$, and the computation decomposes into independent small dense matrix operations. This structure enables tile-size-dependent GPU kernels, cross-layer batching, memory-bounded chunking, and runtime tile-size schedules. Experiments on transformer training and controlled matrix-function diagnostics show that HiMuon improves optimizer-step efficiency while keeping training behavior close to full-matrix Muon in the tested regimes.

AI.tldr: HiMuon is a tiled Newton-Schulz method for Muon-type optimizers that partitions momentum-gradient matrices into independent tiles, reducing computational cost from O(r²sK) to O(HW TK) while preserving training behavior close to full-matrix Muon.
AI.motivation: Muon-type optimizers use full-matrix Newton-Schulz updates that are computationally expensive (O(r²sK)) and couple all rows and columns through Gram matrix products, motivating a more efficient approximation.
AI.method: HiMuon partitions each momentum-gradient matrix into T×T tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map independently to each tile, and reassembles results. This creates a local matrix-function map that preserves spectral interactions within tiles but discards them across tile boundaries.
AI.result: The computational cost decreases from O(r²sK) to O(HW TK). The approach enables tile-size-dependent GPU kernels, cross-layer batching, memory-bounded chunking, and runtime tile-size schedules. Experiments on transformer training and matrix-function diagnostics show improved optimizer-step efficiency with behavior close to full-matrix Muon.
AI.conclusion: HiMuon provides a practical, efficient approximation to full-matrix Muon optimization by leveraging tiling, significantly reducing computational complexity while maintaining comparable training performance in tested regimes.

title: Local well-posedness for fractional regularizations of hyperbolic systems of equations
summary: We establish a local well-posedness theory for a class of hyperbolic quasilinear evolution systems with fractional dissipation and non-local commutators of the fractional Laplacian. The analysis is motivated by the compressible isentropic Navier-Stokes equations with fractional diffusion and the compressible Euler alignment system with singular communication weights. Our approach does not rely on the cancellation condition previously used to recover the coercivity of the highest-order non-local terms. Instead, we exploit the self-adjointness of the fractional Laplacian together with an elementary algebraic identity for commutators of Fourier multipliers. Combined with the non-homogeneous Littlewood-Paley theory, these ingredietns yield the coercive structure and remains applicable for systems with variable coefficients. We further show that the associated solution operator fails to satisfy Banach's contraction principle. To overcome this difficulty, we establish a Hölder continuity estimate of order $\frac{1}{2}$, combine it with the Aubin-Lions compactness theorem and a tail-control argument, that yields the relative compactness of the solution operator, allowing the application of Schauder's fixed point theorem. The resulting theory provides local existence for arbitrary fractional orders $α\in(0,1)$ and identifies the obstacles for the local uniqueness of solutions in the presence of fractional commutators.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a local well-posedness theory for hyperbolic quasilinear evolution systems with fractional dissipation and non-local commutators, applicable to equations like compressible Navier-Stokes with fractional diffusion. By exploiting self-adjointness of the fractional Laplacian and algebraic commutator identities, the authors avoid cancellation conditions. They use Hölder continuity estimates, Aubin-Lions compactness, and Schauder's fixed point theorem (instead of Banach's) to prove existence for any fractional order α∈(0,1), while identifying obstacles to local uniqueness.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the compressible isentropic Navier-Stokes equations with fractional diffusion and the compressible Euler alignment system with singular communication weights, which require a well-posedness theory that handles fractional dissipation and non-local commutators without restrictive cancellation conditions.
AI.method: The method exploits (i) self-adjointness of the fractional Laplacian and an algebraic identity for commutators of Fourier multipliers, (ii) non-homogeneous Littlewood-Paley theory to obtain coercive structure for variable coefficients, (iii) a Hölder continuity estimate of order 1/2 combined with Aubin-Lions compactness and a tail-control argument to achieve relative compactness, and (iv) Schauder's fixed point theorem instead of Banach's contraction principle.
AI.result: Local existence is established for arbitrary fractional orders α∈(0,1). The Banach contraction principle fails for the associated solution operator, but a Schauder fixed point argument succeeds. The obstacles to local uniqueness in the presence of fractional commutators are identified.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a robust local well-posedness framework for fractional dissipative quasilinear systems, overcoming previous reliance on cancellation conditions, and clarifies the limitations regarding solution uniqueness.

title: Quasi-Feynman formulas that provide fast converging Chernoff approximations to solution of parabolic differential equation on the real line
summary: We construct explicit approximations to the solution of a second-order parabolic partial differential equation on the real line with variable coefficients. The method is based on Chernoff's product formula and uses a new operator-valued function defined through proper Riemann integrals over a bounded interval, which makes the approach readily usable in numerical practice. For sufficiently smooth initial data and coefficients, we prove that the resulting Chernoff approximations converge uniformly in space and time with a quadratic rate, improving the standard first-order estimate. The construction yields a new class of quasi-Feynman formulas that are neither grid-based nor Galerkin-type, but instead rely on semigroup theory and multiple bounded integrals. The theoretical findings are validated by symbolic computation, and the paper contributes both refined error bounds and a practical analytical tool for parabolic problems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents explicit approximations for second-order parabolic PDEs using Chernoff's product formula with a new operator-valued function based on bounded Riemann integrals, achieving quadratic convergence rate and introducing quasi-Feynman formulas validated by symbolic computation.
AI.motivation: To improve the standard first-order convergence rate of Chernoff approximations for parabolic PDEs and to develop a practical, grid-free analytical tool that avoids Galerkin-type discretizations.
AI.method: The method uses Chernoff's product formula with a novel operator-valued function defined via proper Riemann integrals over bounded intervals, combined with semigroup theory and multiple bounded integrals.
AI.result: The Chernoff approximations converge uniformly in space and time with a quadratic rate (improving from first-order), and the approach yields new quasi-Feynman formulas.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides refined error bounds and a practical analytical tool for parabolic problems, validated by symbolic computation, with the method being neither grid-based nor Galerkin-type.

title: Painlevé \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral34\relax} and collisionless shock in the defocusing NLS equation with step-like initial data in the transition regions
summary: We consider the Cauchy problem for the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation with step-like initial data. Using the nonlinear steepest descent method, we derive the long-time asymptotic expansion of the solution to the Cauchy problem in three distinct transition regions. In the first two transition regions, the leading-order asymptotics are characterized by Painlevé \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral34\relax}-type formula, while in the third one is a collisionless shock region, the leading-order asymptotics is describedin terms of Riemann theta functions. Our analysis is based on the Riemann-Hilbert formulation associated with the Cauchy problem of the defocusing NLS equation.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the long-time asymptotic behavior of the defocusing NLS equation with step-like initial data, finding three transition regions governed by Painlevé-type formulas and Riemann theta functions using the nonlinear steepest descent method.
AI.motivation: To understand the long-time asymptotic behavior of the defocusing NLS equation with step-like initial data, particularly the solution in different transition regions.
AI.method: Nonlinear steepest descent method applied to the Riemann-Hilbert formulation of the Cauchy problem.
AI.result: Three distinct transition regions identified: two with leading-order asymptotics characterized by Painlevé-type formulas, and a collisionless shock region described by Riemann theta functions.
AI.conclusion: The long-time asymptotic expansion of the defocusing NLS solution exhibits qualitatively different behavior across three transition regions, with special function descriptions in each.

title: Adaptive Iterative Numerical Homogenization for Quasilinear Nonmonotone Elliptic PDE
summary: We propose and analyze an adaptive iterative numerical homogenization method to approximate the solution of a class of quasilinear nonmonotone elliptic problems that is of multiscale nature. The method is based on the technique of the Localized Orthogonal Decomposition (LOD) applied to the linear problems in each step of a Kačanov iteration. In this approach, the multiscale basis is recomputed adaptively in each iteration and a linear problem is solved with this updated multiscale space, where in both steps the nonlinearity is evaluated using the approximation of the previous iteration. As a key component of the proposed approach, we present a locally computable error indicator, which at each iteration identifies the basis functions requiring updates, while previously computed basis functions are retained whenever possible. We provide a priori error estimates and show convergence of the method, requiring only higher integrability of the right-hand side, but no higher differentiability of the solution itself. Furthermore, we discuss how to adapt the proposed adaptive iterative LOD in the context of Newton's method as iteration scheme. Numerical experiments illustrate the theory and validate the applicability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an adaptive iterative numerical homogenization method for quasilinear nonmonotone elliptic multiscale problems using Localized Orthogonal Decomposition within a Kačanov iteration, featuring an error indicator that adaptively updates basis functions and providing convergence guarantees with only higher integrability assumptions.
AI.motivation: To solve quasilinear nonmonotone elliptic problems of multiscale nature efficiently, where traditional methods are computationally expensive due to the need to resolve fine-scale features repeatedly across nonlinear iterations.
AI.method: Combines Localized Orthogonal Decomposition (LOD) with Kačanov iteration; adaptively recomputes multiscale basis functions per iteration using a locally computable error indicator; also discusses adaptation for Newton's method.
AI.result: Provides a priori error estimates, proves convergence requiring only higher integrability of the right-hand side (not higher differentiability of the solution), and validates through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive iterative LOD method is effective and convergent for quasilinear nonmonotone multiscale elliptic problems, with potential extension to Newton-type iterations.

title: Pogorelov type interior $C^2$ estimate for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds
summary: In this paper, we mainly study the interior $C^{2}$ estimates for a class of sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds. For $0\leq l<k< n$, we establish interior $C^{2}$ estimates at the center of a geodesic ball. Let $Ω$ be a bounded domain (with smooth boundary) on Riemannian manifold. For $0\leq l<k\leq n$, we establish Pogorelov type estimates on $Ω$ with the vanishing Dirichlet boundary condition.

AI.tldr: This paper studies interior C² estimates for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds, establishing estimates at geodesic ball centers and Pogorelov type estimates with vanishing Dirichlet boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: To derive interior C² estimates for a class of sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds, which are important for understanding the regularity theory of fully nonlinear elliptic equations in geometric settings.
AI.method: For 0 ≤ l < k < n, interior C² estimates are established at the center of a geodesic ball. For 0 ≤ l < k ≤ n, Pogorelov type estimates are established on a bounded domain Ω with smooth boundary under vanishing Dirichlet boundary conditions.
AI.result: Interior C² estimates for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds are obtained for two cases: at the center of a geodesic ball (0 ≤ l < k < n) and Pogorelov type estimates on bounded domains with vanishing Dirichlet boundary condition (0 ≤ l < k ≤ n).
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes interior C² estimates and Pogorelov type estimates for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds under specified conditions.

title: A modified Euler-Maruyama method to simulate a one-dimensional sticky diffusion
summary: A sticky diffusion is a process that can stick to and detach from a lower-dimensional boundary. A challenge in simulating such a process is in capturing the change in dimension in a dynamically consistent way. We introduce a numerical algorithm to simulate a one-dimensional sticky diffusion, which sticks to and detaches from a point. Our method is a simple modification of the standard Euler-Maruyama scheme, which chooses with some probability between a reflected Euler-Maruyama update and a jump to the sticky point. We show how to choose this probability to be consistent with the generator of the desired dynamics, and we prove that our scheme converges weakly to a sticky diffusion with order 1.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a modified Euler-Maruyama scheme that alternates between reflected updates and jumps to simulate a one-dimensional sticky diffusion at a point, with proven first-order weak convergence.
AI.motivation: Existing numerical methods struggle to capture the dimensional change (from 1D to 0D) when a diffusion process sticks to or detaches from a lower-dimensional boundary in a dynamically consistent way.
AI.method: A simple modification of the standard Euler-Maruyama scheme: at each step, choose with a specific probability between a reflected Euler-Maruyama update and a direct jump to the sticky point. The probability is chosen to be consistent with the generator of the sticky diffusion dynamics.
AI.result: The proposed scheme converges weakly to the sticky diffusion with order 1 (first-order weak convergence).
AI.conclusion: The simple probabilistic modification of the Euler-Maruyama scheme effectively captures the sticky diffusion dynamics, providing a provably convergent numerical method for this challenging multi-dimensional behavior.

title: Local stability for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities
summary: We establish a local stability result for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities. Given the solution $u$ of the Dirichlet torsion problem in a domain $Ω$, we consider shape functionals $\mathcal{J}(Ω)$ involving the integral of $j(u)$, where $j$ is convex and satisfies suitable structural assumptions. By Talenti's comparison principle, balls maximize $\mathcal{J}$ among sets of prescribed measure. We prove that this extremal property is stable in the class of nearly spherical sets: the deficit from the optimal value controls the square of the $H^{1/2}$-norm of the boundary perturbation. The argument relies on shape derivative techniques, including the computation of the second variation and the introduction of an adjoint state. As applications, the result covers several relevant examples, including the torsional rigidity, $L^p$-norms of the torsion function for $p\ge 2$, and Moser-Trudinger functional in dimension two.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities, the optimality of balls is stable under nearly spherical perturbations, with the deficit controlling the $H^{1/2}$-norm of the boundary perturbation.
AI.motivation: To establish stability results for shape optimization problems involving convex functionals of the torsion function, extending the known extremal property that balls maximize such functionals.
AI.method: The authors use shape derivative techniques, compute the second variation of the shape functional, and introduce an adjoint state to analyze nearly spherical perturbations.
AI.result: They prove that the deficit from the optimal value (ball) controls the square of the $H^{1/2}$-norm of the boundary perturbation, establishing local stability for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities.
AI.conclusion: The result applies to several important examples including torsional rigidity, $L^p$-norms of the torsion function for $p\ge 2$, and the Moser-Trudinger functional in dimension two.

title: Automated Galerkin time stepping in Irksome
summary: As the study of temporal and spatial discretization schemes continues to advance, recent work has focused on the use of Galerkin-in-time discretization schemes that enable broader structure-preservation than is known for Runge-Kutta integrators. While the promise of such discretizations is immense, their realization has, until now, generally relied on bespoke implementations that have limited their wider use. In this work, we present automation in Irksome for both discontinuous Galerkin and continuous Petrov-Galerkin time stepping of semidiscrete variational problems. The implementation supports auxiliary variables, flexible temporal quadrature, and monolithic algebraic solvers, and it enables switching between Runge-Kutta and Galerkin-in-time formulations with minimal changes to user code. Numerical examples illustrate accuracy, solver performance, and structure preservation across representative PDE systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Irksome, a software tool that automates both discontinuous Galerkin and continuous Petrov-Galerkin time-stepping methods, enabling users to switch between Runge-Kutta and Galerkin-in-time formulations with minimal code changes while supporting auxiliary variables, flexible quadrature, and monolithic solvers.
AI.motivation: Galerkin-in-time discretization schemes offer superior structure-preservation compared to Runge-Kutta integrators, but their adoption has been limited due to the need for bespoke, custom implementations.
AI.method: The authors develop automation in the Irksome framework for both discontinuous Galerkin (DG) and continuous Petrov-Galerkin (CPG) time stepping of semidiscrete variational problems, with support for auxiliary variables, flexible temporal quadrature, and monolithic algebraic solvers.
AI.result: Numerical examples demonstrate accuracy, solver performance, and structure preservation across representative PDE systems, with minimal code changes required to switch between Runge-Kutta and Galerkin-in-time formulations.
AI.conclusion: The automation lowers the barrier to using Galerkin-in-time methods, enabling wider adoption of structure-preserving time discretizations without requiring bespoke implementations.

title: Instability of periodic waves in generalized Korteweg-de Vries-Burgers equation with monostable source
summary: We study periodic traveling waves in a general class of nonlinear dispersive equations with Burgers dissipation and a monostable Fisher-KPP reaction source. Under natural assumptions on the dispersion symbol, we prove the existence of small-amplitude periodic traveling waves bifurcating from a constant equilibrium by means of a Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction. We then investigate the spectral stability of these waves by combining Floquet-Bloch decomposition with perturbation theory for linear operators. It is shown that the Floquet spectrum of the linearized operator intersects the unstable complex half-plane, implying spectral instability with respect to localized perturbations. The results recover the recently established instability theory for the Korteweg-de Vries-Burgers-Fisher equation as a special case and provide a unified framework for a broad class of local and nonlocal dispersive systems. These results reveal a common instability mechanism generated by the interplay of dispersion, Burgers dissipation, and monostable reaction dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that small-amplitude periodic traveling waves in a broad class of nonlinear dispersive equations with Burgers dissipation and Fisher-KPP reaction are spectrally unstable due to the interplay of dispersion, dissipation, and reaction dynamics.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified instability theory for periodic traveling waves in dispersive-dissipative-reaction systems, generalizing previous results for the Korteweg-de Vries-Burgers-Fisher equation to a broader class of local and nonlocal dispersive models.
AI.method: Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction for existence of small-amplitude periodic waves; Floquet-Bloch decomposition combined with perturbation theory for linear operators to analyze spectral stability.
AI.result: The Floquet spectrum of the linearized operator intersects the unstable complex half-plane, indicating spectral instability to localized perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The instability mechanism is universal across a wide class of dispersive systems, driven by the interplay of dispersion, Burgers dissipation, and monostable reaction.

title: \chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation
summary: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

AI.tldr: CHISAO is a GPU-native population optimizer that finds all modes of multimodal black-box functions by running batches in parallel and using a convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle. It achieves 100% mode recovery on all 42 SFU benchmark functions across dimensions 2-64, with up to 39x speedup over CPU baselines, and remains robust under substantial likelihood noise.
AI.motivation: Finding all modes of multimodal black-box functions is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent) operate sequentially and cannot exploit modern GPU hardware parallelism.
AI.method: CHISAO (Convergence-Halt-Invert-Stick-And-Oscillate) runs an entire sample batch simultaneously on GPU. It uses a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle: samples reaching true peaks are frozen ("stuck") and preserved, while others keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster strategies maintains population diversity.
AI.result: On all 42 SFU benchmark functions across d∈{2,4,8,16,32,64}, CHISAO achieves 100% mode recovery where CPU baselines collapse at d≥8. Up to 34x speedup over basin-hopping on Michalewicz d=64, up to 39x on Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid d=64 (unimodal). Under likelihood noise σ_noise up to 1.0, mode detection remains 100% reliable.
AI.conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates that GPU-native population optimization with deliberate oscillation cycles can dramatically outperform sequential CPU baselines for multimodal black-box optimization, achieving perfect mode recovery at high dimensions with substantial speedups and noise robustness.

title: A Quasi-Variational--Hemivariational Inequality for the Convective Brinkman--Forchheimer Extended Darcy Equations with Bingham Fluids
summary: This paper is devoted to the analysis of a quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality associated with the convective Brinkman--Forchheimer extended Darcy (CBFeD) equations for Bingham fluids in both two and three spatial dimensions. The considered model describes incompressible fluid flow through porous media while incorporating convection, nonlinear damping effects, and Forchheimer-type resistance, together with non-smooth and non-convex slip boundary conditions. We first derive an appropriate weak formulation of the problem, which leads naturally to a Bingham-type quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality with a velocity-dependent constraint set. By employing the Kakutani--Ky Fan fixed point theorem, we establish the existence of weak solutions for the resulting multivalued quasi-variational inequality formulation of the CBFeD system. Furthermore, we prove that every weak solution of the associated quasi-variational inequality is also a solution of the corresponding quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality. The analysis presented in this work provides a rigorous mathematical framework for CBFeD models with Bingham fluids under non-monotone and non-smooth boundary interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the existence of weak solutions for a quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality modeling Bingham fluid flow through porous media (CBFeD equations) using the Kakutani--Ky Fan fixed point theorem, and shows equivalence between the quasi-variational inequality and quasi-variational--hemivariational formulations.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical framework for the CBFeD model with Bingham fluids under non-smooth, non-convex slip boundary conditions, which had not been previously established.
AI.method: The authors derive a weak formulation leading to a Bingham-type quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality with a velocity-dependent constraint set, then apply the Kakutani--Ky Fan fixed point theorem to prove existence of weak solutions for the multivalued quasi-variational inequality formulation.
AI.result: Existence of weak solutions for the CBFeD system is established; additionally, every weak solution of the associated quasi-variational inequality is proven to also solve the original quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for CBFeD models with Bingham fluids under non-monotone and non-smooth boundary interactions in both 2D and 3D.

title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
summary: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper proves theoretically that Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) can achieve zero-shot size transfer—training on small graphs and deploying on larger ones—by establishing convergence rates of GNDE solutions to their infinite-size graphon limits, and validates these rates through experiments.
AI.motivation: To provide a quantitative theoretical foundation for the zero-shot size-transfer principle of GNDEs, which has been observed empirically but lacked rigorous mathematical justification, especially on sparse random graphs with graphon structure.
AI.method: The authors introduce Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits, prove well-posedness, derive trajectory-wise convergence rates of $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$ for GNDEs to Graphon-NDEs, analyze DTO vs. OTD training discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, and validate with HSBM/tent graphon experiments and zero-shot transfer across four graphon classes.
AI.result: Proved convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$ with high probability; showed DTO and OTD consistency with discrepancies $O(1/M)$ (hidden-state) and $O(1/M^2)$ (parameter-gradients); experiments confirmed theoretical rates and demonstrated successful zero-shot transfer on larger graphs.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous theoretical grounding for zero-shot size transfer in GNDEs, showing that models trained on small graphs can be deployed on larger graphs with quantifiable guarantees, bridging graph neural differential equations and graphon theory.

title: Gelfand-Shilov spaces and operators with ultradifferential weighted symbols on non-compact manifolds
summary: We invariantly define Gelfand-Shilov spaces on classes of non-compact manifolds with a certain ``structure at infinity''. We also construct and study a global calculus of pseudodifferential operators on such manifolds, locally defined by symbols satisfying estimates associated with weight sequences. The operators so obtained act naturally on the previously defined Gelfand-Shilov spaces. These generalise analogous functional spaces and operators defined on Euclidean spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper defines Gelfand-Shilov spaces on non-compact manifolds with specific structure at infinity, develops a global pseudodifferential operator calculus on these manifolds, and shows these operators naturally act on the defined spaces, generalizing Euclidean counterparts.
AI.motivation: To extend Gelfand-Shilov spaces and associated pseudodifferential operator calculus from Euclidean spaces to non-compact manifolds with appropriate structure at infinity.
AI.method: Invariant definitions of Gelfand-Shilov spaces on non-compact manifolds; construction of global pseudodifferential operator calculus using symbols with weight sequence estimates.
AI.result: Gelfand-Shilov spaces are invariantly defined; a global pseudodifferential calculus is constructed; operators act naturally on these spaces.
AI.conclusion: Provides generalization of functional spaces and operators from Euclidean spaces to non-compact manifolds.

title: Preconditioning Magnetic Systems in Kohn-Sham Density Functional Theory
summary: The convergence of the self-consistent field iterations in Kohn-Sham density functional theory can be significantly hindered by the presence of small eigenvalues in the dielectric matrix, which are often associated with electronic phase transitions in magnetic systems. In this work, we study this type of convergence issues and propose a new preconditioning scheme to mitigate them. Our preconditioning scheme is inspired by the Stoner model and based on a non-interacting susceptibility that neglects orbital variations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a range of ferromagnetic systems, showing that it can significantly reduce the number of iterations required to achieve convergence in the vicinity of magnetic phase transitions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new preconditioning scheme inspired by the Stoner model to improve self-consistent field iteration convergence in Kohn-Sham density functional theory for magnetic systems near phase transitions.
AI.motivation: Small eigenvalues in the dielectric matrix hinder convergence of self-consistent field iterations in Kohn-Sham DFT, particularly in magnetic systems near electronic phase transitions.
AI.method: A preconditioning scheme inspired by the Stoner model, based on a non-interacting susceptibility that neglects orbital variations.
AI.result: The scheme significantly reduces the number of iterations needed for convergence in ferromagnetic systems near magnetic phase transitions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Stoner-inspired preconditioner effectively mitigates convergence issues related to magnetic phase transitions in Kohn-Sham DFT.

title: A $Tb$ type theorem for suppressed kernels
summary: In this article, a non-homogeneous $Tb$ type theorem for arbitrary dimensional Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators is proved. This is an extension of an analogous non-homogeneous $Tb$ theorem for the Cauchy transform, in the planar setting, due to Nazarov, Treil and Volberg. The novelties of the present work are the change of dimension and the fact that the operators to which the theorem applies are not necessarily antisymmetric. The techniques used in the proof include, among others, suppressed kernels, decompositions in $L^2(μ)$, where $μ$ is a Radon measure in $\mathbb{R}^d$, and a probabilistic argument resulting from taking averages of the operators involved.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a non-homogeneous Tb theorem for Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators in arbitrary dimensions, extending prior work on the Cauchy transform in the plane.
AI.motivation: To extend the non-homogeneous Tb theorem beyond the planar Cauchy transform to higher dimensions and to operators that are not necessarily antisymmetric.
AI.method: Techniques include suppressed kernels, L²(μ) decompositions for Radon measures μ in ℝ^d, and probabilistic arguments via averaging of operators.
AI.result: A non-homogeneous Tb type theorem is proved for arbitrary-dimensional Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators.
AI.conclusion: The theorem generalizes previous results by relaxing dimensional and symmetry constraints, broadening the applicability of non-homogeneous Tb theory.

title: Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation
summary:
Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that neural network expressivity should be understood not only through regularity-based basis expansion, but also through algorithmic complexity—NNs can emulate real-valued circuits, and any definable NN with non-affine nonlinearities and a parallelization condition is a universal approximator. It provides guarantees for various function classes and shows NNs can numerically emulate algorithms like Newton-Raphson and power iteration, with precision demonstrated via exponential improvement in shortest-path computation.
AI.motivation: The standard perspective on NN expressivity (emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes) captures complexity primarily through regularity, failing to distinguish intuitively simple vs. complicated objects with comparable regularity (e.g., square-root function vs. Brownian path). The paper aims to fill this gap by proposing NNs as models of computation.
AI.method: The authors view NNs as real-valued circuits over a prescribed elementary gate language. They establish that if a function is computable by such a circuit, an NN can approximate it with explicit depth, width, and parameter bounds controlled by the circuit's structure. They also prove that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition (allowing non-linearities like attention or layer normalization) is a universal approximator iff it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
AI.result: Universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and NN emulation of numerical algorithms (Newton-Raphson, power iteration). Key precision result: shortest-path on k-vertex graphs yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving over the generic O(ε^{-c k^2}) Lipschitz-approximation scale.
AI.conclusion: Neural-network complexity is governed not only by regularity but also by algorithmic complexity.
