This computational perspective unifies and extends existing approximation theory, providing tighter bounds for structured problems and broadening the applicability of NN approximation guarantees.

title: Evolving edge weights via local entropy flow and cohesion flow on graphs
summary: In this paper, we first propose two different quantities on graphs, namely local entropy and cohesion, then design two corresponding flows for edge weights: the local entropy flow and the cohesion flow. We establish the global existence and uniqueness of solutions for both flows and investigate their asymptotic behaviors, including the case that the limit goes to positive infinity. Moreover, they can be applied to fundamental network analysis tasks, including community detection and node classification. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves performance competitive with Ollivier Ricci flow and Lin-Lu-Yau Ricci flow on benchmark network analysis tasks. In experimental scenarios, we first apply the cohesion flow to evolve the edge weights of the graph, and then apply the local entropy flow to further update the resulting weighted graph. Both flows are computationally efficient, leading to a significant reduction in overall computational cost and improved scalability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces two new graph quantities (local entropy and cohesion) and their corresponding flows for edge weights, proving existence, uniqueness, and asymptotic behaviors. The method is applied to network analysis tasks like community detection and node classification, achieving competitive performance with Ricci flow methods while being more computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: To develop novel graph-theoretic quantities and flows that can effectively analyze network structures (community detection, node classification) with better computational efficiency compared to existing Ricci flow methods.
AI.method: Proposes local entropy flow and cohesion flow for edge weights on graphs; applies cohesion flow first to evolve edge weights, then local entropy flow to refine the weighted graph.
AI.result: Competitive performance with Ollivier Ricci flow and Lin-Lu-Yau Ricci flow on benchmark tasks; significant reduction in computational cost and improved scalability.
AI.conclusion: Local entropy and cohesion flows are viable, efficient alternatives to Ricci flow methods for network analysis, with rigorous theoretical foundations.

title: Holistic Multivariance Decomposition: Adapting Mode Interrelations in Low-Rank Tensor Approximations
summary: Low-rank tensor approximation is a foundational tool for multidimensional data analysis in scientific computing, classically dominated by Tucker and Canonical Polyadic (CP) decompositions. While widely adopted, these standard approximation schemes represent data as sums of rank-1 tensors formed via mode-wise outer products. This inherent mathematical structure captures the independent variations of individual modes but systematically neglects the mutual interactions and coupled dimensional interdependencies natively embedded within the tensor. To overcome this structural limitation, we introduce the Holistic Multivariance Decomposition (HMD) framework. HMD provides a novel tensor decomposition algorithm that explicitly models both isolated mode effects and higher order mutual relationships through specialized projection operators. Numerical evaluations focusing on three distinct benchmarks from various fields demonstrate that the proposed HMD framework consistently yields significantly lower reconstruction errors compared to both Tucker and CP decomposition. These results establish HMD as a robust, high fidelity computational method for resolving complex, deeply coupled multidimensional data structures in science and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Holistic Multivariance Decomposition (HMD), a new tensor decomposition framework that models both isolated mode effects and higher-order mutual relationships, outperforming traditional Tucker and CP decompositions on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Classical tensor decompositions (Tucker & CP) capture only independent mode variations via mode-wise outer products, systematically neglecting mutual interactions and coupled dimensional interdependencies embedded within tensors.
AI.method: The Holistic Multivariance Decomposition (HMD) framework uses specialized projection operators to explicitly model both isolated mode effects and higher-order mutual relationships, overcoming the structural limitations of Tucker and CP decompositions.
AI.result: On three distinct benchmarks from various fields, HMD consistently yields significantly lower reconstruction errors compared to both Tucker and CP decomposition.
AI.conclusion: HMD is a robust, high-fidelity computational method for resolving complex, deeply coupled multidimensional data structures in science and engineering applications.

title: Numerical Approximation for Path-Dependent McKean-Vlasov Control with Non-Asymptotic Error Estimates
summary: Path-dependent McKean--Vlasov (MKV) control models large interacting populations with history-dependent dynamics and costs. This paper develops a unified approximation-and-learning framework for continuous time path-dependent MKV problem under open-loop controls. First, an Euler discretization scheme with piecewise-constant controls is shown to achieve a non-asymptotic error of $O(h^{1/4})$. Second, we establish a discrete dynamic programming principle and prove value equivalence between open-loop and history-dependent feedback controls, enabling optimization on a reduced filtration. Third, an interacting particle system is introduced to approximate the continuous-time value, yielding an overall error bound of $O(h^{1/4}) + O(M^{-γ})$ for $M$ particles and an explicitly given $γ> 0$. Finally, we propose a fully implementable neural-network policy-gradient method using pathwise features. Numerical experiments, including a path-dependent linear-quadratic benchmark, demonstrate the effectiveness of the algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a unified approximation-and-learning framework for path-dependent McKean-Vlasov control problems under open-loop controls, with theoretical error bounds and a neural-network-based algorithm.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of controlling large interacting populations with history-dependent dynamics and costs, where standard Markovian assumptions break down.
AI.method: Euler discretization with piecewise-constant controls (O(h^{1/4}) error), discrete dynamic programming with equivalence between open-loop and history-dependent feedback controls, interacting particle system approximation (O(M^{-γ}) error), and a neural-network policy-gradient method.
AI.result: Overall error bound of O(h^{1/4}) + O(M^{-γ}) for M particles with explicitly given γ>0; numerical validation on a path-dependent linear-quadratic benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides theoretically grounded, implementable algorithms for path-dependent MKV problems, with controlled approximation errors.

title: Valuing American options and Flexible Forwards contracts in time-dependent models
summary: A flexible forward (FF) is a customized FX hedging instrument that guarantees a fixed exchange rate while letting the holder choose the delivery date within a pre-agreed window. It is therefore an American-style option on timing, and its valuation must respect the volatility skew of the underlying currency pair. We price FF contracts (and, more generally, American options) under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model which captures the forward-skew term structure while preserving analytical tractability through a recursive (matrix) Riccati solution for the joint characteristic function. Extending the integral-equation (decomposition) approach to time-dependent coefficients, we derive a Volterra equation characterizing the early-exercise surface. The expectation in the decomposition formula is evaluated by two complementary spectral methods: a double cosine (COS) expansion of the transition density, and a damped-Sinc (DSINC) local-basis scheme that is more accurate and stays robust when a low Feller ratio or large vol-of-vol induces Gibbs oscillations in the COS series. Benchmarked against a penalty-iteration MCS-ADI finite-difference solver, both methods price a contract in about 1-2 seconds, roughly an order of magnitude faster than the finest finite-difference grid, while DSINC improves median accuracy over COS by about a factor of twelve. The experiments also show that the early-exercise surface is a substantially nonlinear function of the variance, contrary to the linear-in-variance approximation common in earlier work.

AI.tldr: This paper prices Flexible Forward FX contracts (American-style options on timing) under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model using recursive Riccati solutions and two spectral methods (COS and DSINC), achieving 1-2 second pricing (~10x faster than finite-difference) with DSINC being ~12x more accurate than COS.
AI.motivation: Flexible Forwards are customized FX hedging instruments lacking efficient valuation methods that respect volatility skew and forward-skew term structure. Prior work used linear-in-variance approximations which are inaccurate.
AI.method: Extended integral-equation decomposition to time-dependent coefficients under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model; derived Volterra equation for early-exercise boundary; solved via two spectral methods: COS (double cosine expansion) and DSINC (damped-Sinc local basis).
AI.result: Both methods price contracts in ~1-2 seconds (order of magnitude faster than finest finite-difference grid); DSINC improves median accuracy over COS by ~12x; early-exercise surface is substantially nonlinear in variance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral methods provide fast and accurate pricing for Flexible Forwards under realistic stochastic volatility, and the linear-in-variance approximation commonly used is inadequate.

title: Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers
summary: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ENS uses the PDE residual as an input (not optimization target) to iteratively correct predictions, achieving better accuracy and efficiency than hybrid methods, especially in ill-conditioned regimes.
AI.motivation: Hybrid methods that minimize PDE residuals via gradient descent are unreliable for ill-conditioned systems—low residual ≠ high accuracy—and inherit high compute costs.
AI.method: ENS feeds the PDE residual field as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling the model to learn an update policy that iteratively corrects its own predictions based on the spatial structure of errors.
AI.result: ENS achieves highest accuracy across 4 PDE families (up to 10× on turbulent Kolmogorov flow), generalizes zero-shot under distribution shift, and outperforms hybrid methods most in ill-conditioned regimes.
AI.conclusion: Using residuals as input rather than optimization target is more effective and efficient; ENS avoids costly hybrid optimization while being more robust in challenging regimes.
